articles.title: Challenges for building up confirmatory science in lab biology: lessons learned from the Brazilian Reproducibility Initiative
articles.authors: Olavo Bohrer Amaral; Bruna Valério; Clarissa França Dias Carneiro; Gabriel Paz Souza Mota; Kleber Neves; Mariana Abreu; Pedro Batista Tan
articles.abstract: The Brazilian Reproducibility Initiative was a nationwide systematic replication project to assess the replicability of biomedical experiments published by Brazilian research groups. Beyond its results, the Initiative offered a privileged window to observe academic labs performing replications with a confirmatory mindset that is not usual for most researchers in the life sciences. In this article, we outline the main challenges met by the Initiative’s coordinating team, both in terms of implementing confirmatory methods in lab biology and of running large-scale collaborations within an academic environment. Our conclusions suggest steps that can be undertaken by multiple actors, from individual researchers to policymakers, in order to improve the replicability of biomedical research.

articles.title: p-Uniform* is an Estimator of the Three-Parameter Selection Model: Commentary on van Aert and van Assen (2026)
articles.authors: James E Pustejovsky
articles.abstract: Van Aert and Van Assen (2026; https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-025-02812-4) introduced a novel method called p-uniform* for estimating average effect size in meta-analyses subject to publication bias. They characterized the method as a distinct, more parsimonious model than the well-known three-parameter selection model (3PSM) of Hedges (1992; https://doi.org/10.1214/ss/1177011364). I argue that p-uniform* is not a distinct model, but rather a different estimator of the average effect size and heterogeneity parameters of the 3PSM. The p-uniform* estimator coincides with the maximum likelihood estimator of the 3PSM when all included effects have identical sampling variances; otherwise, the estimators differ. Before p-uniform* is used in applications, its theoretical rationale should be clarified and its empirical performance should be more thoroughly explored through simulations under realistic data-generating processes, especially ones involving primary studies that vary in sample size.

articles.title: Self-compassion and mental wellbeing: a baseline cross-construct map from a multilingual Erasmus+ youth-sector sample in Greece and France
articles.authors: Anastasia Charitidou; Ioannis Valasakis
articles.abstract:
This is the companion paper to the design and feasibility evaluation of Compassion is Sexy (CIS), a 12–14 hour Erasmus+ KA210-YOU Small-scale Partnership compassion curriculum delivered across French-, Greek- and English-speaking workshop contexts during 2025–2026 (comPASSION is sexy, Project Code: 2024-3-FR02-KA210-YOU-000295806; no external research-ethics committee review; evaluation adheres to Erasmus+ quality standards for youth-sector practice). Where the intervention paper reports the dimensional pre–post pattern of change, the present analysis is restricted to the baseline phase and asks a single, prior question: how do the six factors of self-compassion relate to the three factors of mental wellbeing in this multilingual non-clinical adult sample?

The programme was delivered under the Erasmus+ KA210-YOU fewer-opportunities target framing; in 211 usable baseline participants (Greek site n = 105, including sizeable subgroups born outside Greece; French site n = 106, similarly multinational; roughly one third of respondents self-identified with at least one Erasmus+ fewer-opportunities or marginalised-status category), all six SCS-26 subscales were positively associated with the MHC-SF total (ρ = 0.18–0.34, all 95% CIs above zero except common humanity which crossed zero on the social cell). The strongest pooled pair was SCS total × MHC psychological (ρ = 0.42, CI [0.30, 0.52]). Across the 6×3 sub-by-sub matrix the pattern is selective rather than uniform: the self-relating SCS factors (self-kindness, self-judgmentR, isolationR, over-identificationR) load broadly across MHC dimensions, whereas the more cognitive-social SCS factors (common humanity, mindfulness) correlate reliably only with the psychological MHC dimension and show CIs that include zero for the emotional and social dimensions (Fig. 1, Table 1).

Baseline country contrasts indicate higher self-compassion in the Greek-site cohort (d = 0.45, CI [0.17, 0.74]) on SCS total and on four of six subscales, with MHC totals comparable; this baseline non-equivalence argues for a country covariate in any pooled mixed-effects reanalysis. The same architecture also held when the matrix was recomputed within fewer-opportunities (n = 69) and without fewer-opportunities status (n = 138) subgroups: no cell showed 95% CI separation, supporting the cross-construct map as a shared feature of the sample rather than an artefact of marginalised-status composition.

This is a baseline, observational, cross-sectional map; no causal or mechanistic claim is made. The architecture is compatible with a theoretical reading in which compassionate self-relating is more tightly coupled to eudaimonic (psychological) than to hedonic (emotional) wellbeing in this population, and provides effect-size anchors against which future multilingual deliveries can be planned.

Keywords: self-compassion subscales; MHC-SF; eudaimonic vs. hedonic wellbeing; cross-construct correlations; baseline; Greece; France

articles.title: Cross-Linguistic Factors in Early Numerical Development
articles.authors: Urvi Maheshwari; David Barner
articles.abstract: What role does language play in exact numerical reasoning? We argue that natural language represents number at multiple levels that vary cross-linguistically – in grammatical morpho-syntax, in number words, and in rule-governed counting routines. In this chapter, we first describe how cross-linguistic variability in grammatical structure impacts our representations of countable individuals and sets (Part I). Next, we review variability in how languages represent small numbers (Part II). And finally, we describe cross-linguistic variability in the syntactic structure of counting systems (Part III). We suggest that cross-linguistic research provides an important window into how humans come to reason about exact magnitudes, and conclude with a discussion of outstanding questions and avenues for future research.

articles.title: A short embodied compassion curriculum for the Erasmus+ youth sector: design, feasibility, and dimensional pre-post patterns of Compassion is Sexy in Greece and France.
articles.authors: Anastasia Charitidou; Ioannis Valasakis
articles.abstract:
Compassion is Sexy (CIS) is a 12–14 hour, four-session experiential curriculum integrating self-compassion practices, embodied and arts-based methods, and group dialogue for mixed adult audiences (youth workers, facilitators, NGO staff, young adults) in non-formal Erasmus+ settings. The programme was delivered across French-, Greek-, and English-speaking workshop contexts during 2025–2026 in Greece and France within the Erasmus+ KA210-YOU Small-scale Partnership project comPASSION is sexy (Project Code: 2024-3-FR02-KA210-YOU-000295806). The evaluation was conducted within a low-risk, practice-based youth-work framework incorporating informed consent, voluntary participation, anonymisation, and data-minimisation procedures. This is an uncontrolled observational feasibility evaluation; pre–post statistics reflect within-person associations rather than causal effects.

From 288 intake records across 23 site-level CSV exports, 223 unique participants were identified after email-based linkage, of whom 211 had usable baseline data (Greece n = 105; France n = 106) and 56 returned a usable post-form (Greece n = 50; France n = 6; overall completion 27%). Participants completed the SCS-26 and MHC-SF. Scoring used item means with reverse coding for negative SCS items and 50% prorating thresholds for MHC subscales. One French cohort of five participants recorded no psychometrics due to placeholder export headers, and three English-language forms omitted MHC item 7.

At the headline level, the programme was associated with a moderate positive shift in total self-compassion (Mpre = 2.98, Mpost = 3.31; Cohen’s dz = 0.54, 95% CI [0.30, 0.85]) and a smaller positive shift in total mental wellbeing (dz = 0.42, CI [0.17, 0.70]); the Greek-only subsample closely tracked the pooled estimates. Five of six SCS-26 subscales shifted in the direction of greater self-compassion (self-kindness dz = 0.52, common humanity 0.43, mindfulness 0.40, over-identificationR 0.44, self-judgmentR 0.38), whereas isolation showed no reliable change (dz = 0.12, 95% CI [−0.14, 0.39]). Within the MHC-SF, change concentrated in the social (dz = 0.45) and psychological (dz = 0.38) subscales, while the emotional subscale showed a smaller change with confidence intervals crossing zero (dz = 0.23, CI [−0.03, 0.49]). Attrition differed by age and number of comfortably spoken languages but was balanced on baseline self-compassion (d = 0.11, p = 0.46), suggesting that the observed SCS shift is unlikely to be fully explained by intake selection on the primary outcome.

These findings support the design and delivery feasibility of a short, embodied, multilingual compassion programme in real Erasmus+ youth-work settings and provide preliminary effect-size estimates for future controlled or waitlist-comparison studies.
Keywords: self-compassion · mental wellbeing · MHC-SF · Erasmus+ · youth work · non-formal education · experiential learning · feasibility study

articles.title: Familiarity with an Artificial Voice Improves Speech-in-Speech Intelligibility
articles.authors: Harriet J Smith; Benjamin Delf; Victor Rosi; Carolyn McGettigan; Emma Holmes
articles.abstract: Artificial voices are encountered frequently in modern life, including artificial “clones” of an individual’s voice. For natural voices, familiar talkers are more intelligible than unfamiliar talkers when competing speech is present, which applies to both naturally-familiar and lab-trained voices. Yet, whether training on an artificial voice produces a similar benefit, and whether this benefit transfers to natural speech from the same talker, is currently unclear. Here, we trained 60 participants to recognise cloned or natural (recorded) versions of 3 different voices. We replicated the familiar-voice benefit to speech intelligibility for natural voices, and found an even larger intelligibility benefit for artificial voices after training. However, training on a voice clone did not improve intelligibility for the natural version of that voice, implying a lack of transfer. Thus, listeners can learn talker-specific cloned voices, which enhances intelligibility in noise, but cloned and natural versions of a person’s voice are not interchangeable.

articles.title: “I know who the bad guy is!” Infants map the word “bad” to hinderers, but not the word “good” to helpers
articles.authors: Francis Yuen; Alexis Black; Kiley Hamlin
articles.abstract: Though a wealth of research has found that young infants have the capacity to evaluate helping and hindering agents (e.g. Hamlin, 2007; for a review, see Woo et al., 2025), it is nevertheless unclear whether these evaluations reflect mere social evaluations or more mature understanding of moral concepts. The present experiments probed the nature of infants’ evaluations by testing whether they associate the morally-relevant words “good” and “bad” with helpers and hinderers. In Exp.1, 32 19-month-olds watched a live puppet show where a protagonist puppet is either helped or hindered by another puppet (ball show, adapted from Hamlin & Wynn, 2011). Then, the infant was presented with the helper and hinderer puppets, and asked, “can you point to the good/bad guy?”. Results revealed that whereas infants reliably mapped “bad” to the hinderer (24/31, p = .003), they showed relatively inconsistent mapping of the word “good” to the helper (p = .09). In Exp.2, 42 20-month-olds watched a video recording of the ball show, then engaged in a preferential looking paradigm in which still images of the helper and hinderer were shown on the screen. An audio prompt then asked, “Where’s the good/bad guy? Find the good/bad guy,” while an eye-tracker monitored infants’ gaze. Results paralleled and bolstered that of Exp.1: infants looked significantly longer at the hinderer when hearing the “bad” prompt, but looked equally at both characters when hearing the “good” prompt. Together, these findings suggest that infants may associate the abstract moral word “bad” with hindering behaviour prior to the second birthday, but the association between “good” and helping behaviour may emerge later. This asymmetry provides further evidence for a negativity bias in social cognition.

articles.title: Using Natural Language Processing to Study Scene Recall in 360° Virtual Reality
articles.authors: Claudia Morales Valiente; Peggy L. St. Jacques
articles.abstract: This study examines how viewing scenes in 2D versus 3D 360° virtual reality affects recall of those scenes. Participants will view immersive scenes presented in both formats and later provide verbal descriptions of what they remember. These recall narratives will be analyzed using natural language processing to examine how the shift from 2D to 3D influences the amount, type, and organization of remembered scene information. The main goal is to determine whether 3D immersive viewing leads to richer or more detailed scene recall compared with 2D viewing. Natural language processing methods will be used to quantify features of participants’ descriptions, including references to objects, spatial layout, actions, perceptual details, and overall descriptive richness. This allows the study to assess not only how much participants remember, but also what kinds of scene information are most affected by the viewing format. By combining 360° virtual reality with computational language analysis, this project aims to better understand how an immersive visual experience shapes memory for complex scenes. The findings may help clarify whether 3D presentation enhances scene encoding and recall, and how these effects are reflected in the language people use to describe remembered environments.

articles.title: Integrating interests, personality, character strengths, and person-job fit in relation to work outcomes
articles.authors: Fabian Gander; Livio Blaser; Rahel Tscharner; Lisa Wagner
articles.abstract:
Objective: Vocational interests, personality traits, and character strengths overlap conceptually and empirically, but their shared versus domain-specific associations with work outcomes – and the role of person-job fit across these domains – are not well described. We examined these domains jointly to capture the interplay among individual differences, perceived job demands, and person–job fit with work outcomes.
Method: We analyzed data from N = 259 working adults (79% women, aged 20-73, Md = 38). Participants self-rated vocational interests (RIASEC), Five-Factor personality dimensions, and VIA character strengths, and, for each domain, rated the importance of these attributes as perceived job demands. Perceived person–job fit was operationalized as correspondence between self-ratings and job-demand ratings within domains. Outcomes were work satisfaction, engagement, and self-rated performance. 
Results: All three domains showed meaningful associations with all outcomes. Effects reflected substantial overlap among domains, alongside smaller domain-specific contributions that differed by outcome. Person-job fit showed small links with satisfaction and engagement—most consistently for vocational interests—whereas associations with self-rated performance were weaker and less consistent across domains.
Conclusion: Interests, personality, and character strengths capture overlapping yet nonidentical aspects of work-related functioning. Integrating strengths with vocational and personality frameworks may inform development-oriented vocational assessment and counseling.

articles.title: Timing-induced illusory percepts of pitch
articles.authors: Jesse Kendall Pazdera; Olive M. Rinaldi; Laurel Trainor
articles.abstract: It has long been proposed that the brain integrates pitch and timing cues during auditory perception. If true, the pitch of a sound should influence its perceived timing, and its timing should influence its perceived pitch. Previous research has found that higher-pitched sounds tend to be perceived as faster than lower-pitched sounds, and in the present study we investigated whether sounds that arrive earlier or later than expected are similarly perceived as higher or lower in pitch. In Experiment 1, participants heard isochronous, repeating standard tones followed by a pitch-shifted probe, and indicated if the pitch increased or decreased. We observed a strong biasing effect of the probe’s timing on its perceived pitch, such that later probes were more likely to be perceived as lower than the standard. Correct, bias-conforming responses to mistimed probes were also significantly faster than responses to on-beat probes. In Experiment 2, we used an adaptive difficulty procedure to investigate whether this timing-induced bias strengthens under conditions of low discriminability. We did not find evidence that bias varies with the magnitude of pitch change or with individual differences in pitch sensitivity. In conjunction with past findings of pitch-induced illusory timing changes, our results support the hypothesis that pitch and time are perceptually integrated. We discuss this integration within a Bayesian predictive coding framework, as possibly learned from real-world correlations between pitch and timing that derive from latent properties of sound sources.

articles.title: Evidence Before Entry: Evaluating the College of Psychologists and Behaviour Analysts of Ontario’s Regulation Modernization Proposals
articles.authors: Jonathan A. Weiss; Larissa Panetta
articles.abstract: This paper critically examines the proposed regulatory changes by the College of Psychologists and Behaviour Analysts of Ontario (CPBAO) to modernize entry-to-practice standards for psychologists and psychological associates in the province. The analysis places Ontario’s changing regulatory environment within the broader Canadian context, comparing provincial standards, training pathways, and workforce availability. Using empirical data, stakeholder feedback, and consensus reports, this manuscript examines evidence to test three implied hypotheses of the proposed changes: 1) they will increase the number of psychologists in Ontario, 2) they will improve access to mental health services, and 3) they will not elevate risk to public safety or reduce practitioner competence. Findings suggest that lowering entry standards and shifting from external accreditation to a college-approved process are unlikely to significantly increase access to mental health care. Evidence does not support the view that competence will remain unchanged among trainees from programs that substantially deviate from accredited models. Removing external evaluative safeguards may increase the likelihood that some less-prepared individuals enter independent practice. An evidence-based, data-driven approach grounded in transparent consultation and continuous evaluation is recommended to ensure that regulatory changes genuinely improve access to competent psychological care across both Ontario and Canada.

articles.title: A cross-cultural psychometric evaluation of the 3-item Relationship Satisfaction Scale across 37 languages
articles.authors: Marta Kowal
articles.abstract:
Objective: To advance research on close relationships, the present study provides a comprehensive psychometric evaluation of the 3-item Relationship Satisfaction Scale from the Perceived Relationship Quality Components (PRQC) inventory and assesses differences in relationship satisfaction across eight culturally and demographically diverse regions.
Background: Romantic relationships are among the most important social relationships humans form. The quality of these relationships shapes many aspects of life, ranging from everyday mood and behavior to health, long-term habits, longevity, and overall life satisfaction. Consequently, the scientific study of romantic relationship quality is of substantial importance. 
Method: Data from 49,876 participants recruited across 149 countries and territories were analyzed. Item-level properties, factor structure, item response theory (IRT) parameters, and measurement invariance, using both traditional approaches and the alignment method, across 37 linguistic versions of the scale were examined. Mean levels of relationship satisfaction were compared across major cultural and geographic regions using a two-level linear mixed-effects framework. 
Results: The findings indicate that the scale is reliable and broadly comparable across culturally diverse samples. All translated versions are publicly available on OSF (https://osf.io/47q6d/overview?view_only=ac5ba45cf89243e39f0a27e888d0fb9a). The results revealed cross-regional and cross-country differences in relationship satisfaction, with individuals from the English-Speaking region reporting the highest levels, while those from Confucian and African-Islamic regions reporting the lowest levels of relationship satisfaction.
Conclusion: Together, these results offer a strong methodological and conceptual foundation for advancing research on relationship quality and provide researchers with robust tools for studying relationship-related constructs across diverse populations speaking 37 different languages.

articles.title: Higher individualism predicts lower intensity of experienced love: Data from 91 countries
articles.authors: Marta Kowal; Piotr Sorokowski; S. Craig Roberts; W. P. Małecki; Michał Misiak; Agnieszka Sorokowska; Adam Bode; Steve M. J. Janssen; Felipe E. García; Simon Ozer
articles.abstract: Love is a near-universal human experience. However, while it transcends cultural boundaries, its intensity varies significantly across individuals and societies. We hypothesize that this variability is partly driven by socio-cultural factors, particularly individualism—a cultural orientation prioritizing personal goals over group interests. As individualism continues to rise globally, its implications for romantic relationships remain underexplored. Here, we address this by examining the link between individualism and love in two large-scale cross-cultural studies. In Study 1, we collected data from a convenience sample of 61,067 partnered individuals from 81 countries. In Study 2, we selected nationally representative samples from 50 countries, with controlled distributions of gender, age, and residential area (N = 6,620). The results were consistent across both studies—as individualism increased, both men and women reported lower levels of experienced love. In Study 2, individualism was additionally linked to a lower expressed desire for love, suggesting that individualism may erode the perceived importance of love. As people become increasingly individualistic, our findings provoke questions about the future of love. They also carry implications for societal challenges such as rising divorce rates, fertility issues, and depression—all of which have been linked to unfulfilled or unhappy love.

articles.title: The Functional Architecture of Self-Regulation: Network-Level Control Parameters of Regulatory Focus in Normative Adult and Adolescent Samples
articles.authors: Timothy J. Strauman; Ann Blandina Brewster
articles.abstract: Regulatory focus theory distinguishes promotion- and prevention-related motivational systems that support adaptive goal pursuit and are implicated in vulnerability to internalizing psychopathology. Prior neuroimaging work has characterized where in the brain these systems are instantiated, but has offered less insight into how they are organized as integrated control networks. We reanalyzed fMRI data from previously published normative adult (N = 48) and normative adolescent (N = 47) samples using a common region-of-interest framework derived from the adult activation maps. Three masks were constructed based on voxel-wise overlap across conditions: a core network capturing voxels active across multiple goal-priming conditions; a promotion-only network capturing promotion-selective processing; and a prevention-only network capturing prevention-selective processing. Within each mask, we derived per-subject parameters indexing system strength and signal differentiation for each motivational domain, and estimated cross-system coupling as the correlations among these parameters across individuals. In both age groups, promotion and prevention magnitudes varied largely independently, and within-system match–mismatch coupling was weak, consistent with a distributed architecture in which promotion and prevention share a representational backbone but operate with substantial differentiation. Adolescents showed significant cross-system coordination of match signals that was absent in adults. These findings support a set of network-level self-regulatory control parameters—strength, differentiation, and cross-system coupling—as a principled vocabulary for the functional architecture of regulatory focus specifically and self-regulation more generally, and provide a normative baseline against which perturbation by adversity, psychopathology, or intervention can be evaluated.

articles.title: Evaluating the Residualized Change Score Pretest-Posttest Mediation Model via the Latent Change Score Specification
articles.authors: Gareth Ong Yu; Amanda Kay Montoya; Yi Feng
articles.abstract: Researchers frequently use statistical mediation analysis to better understand the possible mechanisms through which change occurs in pretest-posttest designs. One common approach is the residualized change score model, in which the posttest mediator and outcome measures are regressed on their pretest values and residuals are used as variables in the mediation model. When implemented with a latent change score (LCS) specification for structural equation modeling, this model is typically estimated with a two-step procedure: posttest-pretest slopes are calculated first and then treated as fixed in the subsequent model. Valente et al. (2021) found that the standard implementation produced conservative Type I error rates, which we successfully replicated. Rates often fell below the lower bound of the recommended robustness interval for the chi-square test. We explore two possible solutions: a df-adjusted model (recalculating fit statistics with 2 fewer df) and a one-step model (estimating the slopes within the LCS model). Through a Monte Carlo simulation, these adjustments are compared against the standard implementation. The df-adjusted model often yielded high Type I error rates. The one-step model produced the closest Type I error rates to the nominal 0.05 and produced more accurate model fit statistics. This one-step procedure is recommended when estimating the residualized change score model to ensure that mediation findings are based on a properly specified model and valid statistical inferences.

articles.title: Beyond Valence: Arousal as a Core Dimension of Affective Polarization
articles.authors: Jakob Kasper; Bert N. Bakker; Yphtach Lelkes; Gijs Schumacher
articles.abstract: Although affective polarization is fundamentally an affective construct, existing theories and measures focus almost exclusively on evaluative valence, largely overlooking emotional arousal. Building on the Circumplex Model of Affect, we develop and evaluate a two-dimensional framework of affective reactions toward political parties and affective polarization that incorporates emotional valence and arousal. Across three studies conducted in the Netherlands (N=984), the United Kingdom (N=1,001), and the United States (N=1,000), we show that feeling thermometers function as measures of valence and that valence and arousal represent empirically distinct dimensions of affective reactions. We then demonstrate that the level of emotional arousal provides incremental validity over traditional, valence-based measures of affective polarization, yielding additional and often stronger associations with key political correlates such as political engagement, ideological extremity, and democratic attitudes. These findings advance the conceptualization and measurement of affective polarization by incorporating arousal as a distinct dimension and clarifying when and why affective polarization is most strongly linked to political behavior and democratic attitudes.

articles.title: The Physical and Mental Metaphors (PMM) task and its validation in schizophrenia: a tool for rapid assessment of figurative language comprehension, between pragmatics and symptoms
articles.authors: Luca Bischetti; Valentina Bambini; Agostoni Giulia; Margherita Bechi; Mariachiara Buonocore; Jacopo Sapienza; Federico Frau; Ginevra Martinelli; Chiara Pompei; Biagio Scalingi
articles.abstract:
Difficulties in figurative language understanding are largely documented as part of a broader pragmatic impairment, especially in psychosis. Among figurative domains, metaphor stands out as a significant predictor of functioning, in addition to exhibiting meaningful associations with psychopathological and cognitive features. Yet, the assessment of metaphor comprehension remains a niche area, also due to the lack of reliable and valid tools. Here, we examined the psychometric properties of a rapid test of metaphor comprehension, the Physical and Mental Metaphors (PMM) task, involving a sample of 143 patients with schizophrenia and 57 controls. 
Findings provide robust support for the PMM reliability and validity. First, internal consistency was satisfactory, and the factorial structure aligned with theoretical assumptions. Second, the PMM demonstrated good validity, being strongly correlated with the pragmatic profile of patients as measured with a standard tool such as the Assessment of Pragmatics and Cognitive Substrates (APACS) test, showing also relevant associations with symptomatology, in particular abstract thinking and the broader domains of disorganization and negative symptoms, as well as weakly with Theory of Mind and global cognition. Third, the PMM task proved highly effective both in discriminating between individuals with and without pragmatic deficit, as detected via APACS, and in distinguishing patients from healthy controls.
Taken together, these results indicate that the PMM task captures a specific impairment in figurative language and the general pragmatic vulnerability characteristic of schizophrenia, at the interface between symptoms. As such, it represents a valuable tool for detecting communicative disorders, characterizing symptoms and complementing the diagnostic process.

articles.title: Systematic review: the integration and interpretation of Social Determinants of Health (SDH) in Digital Phenotyping research (DP)
articles.authors: Tihare Zamorano; Ashley Choucroun; Parnia Akhavansaffar; Marianne Pouliot; Philomène Labilloy; Matthew Raymond; Timothy Friesen; Vincent Paquin; Youcef Barkat; Saiyara Islam
articles.abstract:
Background: Digital phenotyping (DP) for mental health is an emerging field with the potential to become a powerful tool in clinical settings. However, given the risk of bias or incorrect variable interpretation in marginalized subgroups, for this technology to be used effectively, social determinants of health (SDH) must be integrated into DP research. However it is not known how commonly and in what manner SDH are integrated into DP research. 
Methods: We conducted a systematic review of studies applying DP in mental health populations to examine how SDH variables are collected, reported, and integrated. The primary objective was to inform the development of practical, standardized guidelines for incorporating SDH into DP research. Eligible studies used DP in clinical populations defined as diagnosed, symptom-based, or at risk for mental health conditions. We excluded studies focused solely on chronic physical illness, age as a risk indicator, neurodegenerative conditions, or tobacco-only substance use, as well as reviews, protocols, and conceptual papers. We searched PubMed, Embase (OVID), and APA PsycInfo (OVID) on october 4th 2024, Web of Science Core Collection on  October 23rd 2024, and Scopus on October 24th, 2024. Studies were assessed for quality using the JBI Checklist and the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT). (PROSPERO ID: CRD42024626050).
Results: A total of 825 unique papers were screened at the abstract level, 192 underwent full-text review, 74 were accepted for data extraction, and 63 articles were ultimately included in the review.. Considerable heterogeneity was observed in how SDH were reported and integrated into digital phenotyping research. Age, sex, gender, and race/ethnicity were the most frequently reported variables, yet definitions were often inconsistent. Moreover, SDH were most often used solely to describe the characteristics of the target population. Thirty-eight studies (60.3%) used advanced analytic methods, including machine learning. While some studies acknowledged potential demographic or representation biases, formal fairness or bias assessments were rarely conducted. Biases were most often related to age, sex/gender, race/ethnicity, and socioeconomic factors, and behavioral features sometimes had different interpretations across population subgroups. Overall, consideration of bias and the use of fairness or mitigation strategies in DP research remain limited.
Conclusion: This review identified several current gaps in the integration and interpretation of SDH in DP research. Based on our findings, we propose a set of guidelines for incorporating SDH into research projects, organized into three tiers.

articles.title: Evidence for Intergroup Bias in Coalitional but not Selfish Dishonesty
articles.authors: Jareef Martuza; Helge Thorbjørnsen; Jay Joseph Van Bavel; Hallgeir Sjåstad
articles.abstract: People often claim that lying is wrong regardless of whom it helps or harms. Yet in everyday life, dishonesty can serve group interests—protecting allies, advancing teams, or harming rivals. This raises a fundamental question: Is the moral cost of lying fixed, or is it shaped by social identity? Across three experiments, U.S. Democrat and Republican voters (N=5,230) could lie anonymously to double their own or another participant’s earnings. For selfish dishonesty (Experiment 1), people lied equally regardless of whether it harmed their ingroup or outgroup. However, for coalitional dishonesty (Experiment 2)—lying without benefitting personally—people lied 9 percentage-points more to benefit ingroup than outgroup members. Experiment 3 replicated these findings and revealed that people overestimated outgroup dishonesty more than ingroup dishonesty. Experiment 4 (N=866) identified coalitional dishonesty in workplace scenarios. Thus, dishonesty is not only about self-interest versus fixed morality; it is also shaped by selfless group-serving motives.

articles.title: Socially Motivated Observational Learning: Children Preferentially Learn from Overheard Speech Addressed to Their Own Mothers
articles.authors: Sarah Goodman; Ruthe Foushee
articles.abstract: When does overheard speech support early word-learning? The present study provides preliminary evidence that children’s closest social partners (here, mothers) structure attention to overheard speech. In a Tseltal Mayan community where overhearing is central to socialization, 75 mother-child dyads participated in overhearing-sessions exposing them to two novel words. In each session, one child’s mother was directly addressed (“Mother-Addressed” condition), while the second mother merely observed (“Mother-Unaddressed” condition). Immediately after, both children completed two gaze-based tests of word recognition and learning. Results show that, compared to Mother-Unaddressed children, Mother-Addressed children exhibited (i) greater one-shot recognition of the novel word whose referent was visible during the overhearing-session; and (ii) a word-learning advantage on cross-situational familiarization trials . Insofar as who is being spoken to matters, the present study finds that not all overheard input is created equal: rather, results provide preliminary evidence that observational word-learning is socially-structured.

articles.title: Digital Traces of Everyday Smartphone Usage Predict Cognitive Ability
articles.authors: Maximilian Bergmann; Ramona Schoedel; Markus Bühner; Clemens Stachl
articles.abstract: Scalable, objective judgments of individuals’ everyday cognitive functioning may facilitate decision making in key societal domains, including educational, occupational, and healthcare contexts. In a preregistered, theory-informed machine learning approach, we examine how digital traces of everyday smartphone usage allow for predictions of individuals’ general fluid intelligence (Gf), one of the most central human cognitive abilities. Leveraging a German quota sample (N = 367) providing smartphone sensing data of up to six months (1,269,491 phone usages), cross-validated results demonstrate that everyday smartphone usage consistently predicts individuals' Gf scores on a psychometric test (r_Md = .43, r_IQR  = [.35, .51]) with comparable criterion validity. High-dimensional combinations of behaviors (particularly short-term activities related to basic cognitive tasks and dealing with complexity) were predictive for Gf, highlighting how cognitive abilities manifest pervasively yet highly uniquely across individuals. The findings suggest that smartphone-based cognitive judgment may complement traditional cognitive assessments, enhancing early identification of cognitive functioning, fit, and support.

articles.title: Tiny Alchemists: The Developmental Emergence of Empowerment in Exploration
articles.authors: Franziska Brändle; Azzurra Ruggeri; Silja Keßler; Eric Schulz
articles.abstract: How do children's exploration strategies develop? Prior work has shown that children explore more broadly than adults, often driven by uncertainty. Yet, little is known about how more sophisticated strategies, such as empowerment - i.e. seeking actions that expand future possibilities - unfold across development. We investigated this in two experiments using a complex combinatorial game ("Tiny Alchemy''). In Experiment 1, we found that empowerment use increased with age, while uncertainty decreased. Young children (5 - 8 years) primarily relied on uncertainty, while older children (9 - 13 years) and adults used both uncertainty and empowerment. In Experiment 2, we asked whether younger children's limited use of empowerment reflected different semantic intuitions. Results showed that older children used empowerment but could not explicitly judge it, while adults both used and accurately assessed empowerment. These findings suggest that empowerment emerges gradually: first as an implicit strategy in late childhood, and only later becoming explicit and verbalizable.

articles.title: From Agent-Based Opinion Models to Quantum Circuits: A Tutorial
articles.authors: Rahel Geppert; Hedwig Körfgen
articles.abstract:
This work describes and assesses fundamental quantum computing concepts from the perspective of the social sciences. Quantum computers differ fundamentally from classical computers in that their elementary units, qubits, can exist in superpositions of multiple states simultaneously. This
property makes them particularly suited for simulating systems with a large number of interacting components, a promising perspective across disciplines.
In this tutorial, we introduce (i) what properties a social dynamic model should have to be easily translatable into quantum computer mechanics, (ii) eleven steps that have to be completed to achieve that translation and (iii) a practical example in which we illustrate the process.

articles.title: Beyond Reward and Punishment:  Introducing Costly Countercontrol Into the Study of Altruism
articles.authors: Sabine Windmann
articles.abstract: Behavioral accounts of altruism have focused on costly reward and costly punishment. A third facet is expressed here as costly countercontrol, defined as persistent counteraction of dominant reinforcement schemes for the benefit of others. While this form of behavior is inherently risky, it involves behavioral reversal, and if it spreads, it can trigger long-term collective benefits through sociocultural transformation. In a game scenario, it could be operationalized as establishment of other-benefiting behavioral rules despite socially enforced contingencies to the opposite. Different from moral and civil courage, it is conceptualized here from a behaviorist standpoint. Examples from real life include whistleblowing and principled dissent in support of a moral cause. The article explores the challenges of assessing such behavior and reviews selected paradigms that contain its main components. Lastly, the limitations of current artificial intelligence systems in generating countercontrol are discussed, thereby highlighting the uniquely human capacity for this antagonistic form of altruism.

articles.title: Meaningfulness Facilitates Voluntary Attentional Retrieval in Visual Working Memory
articles.authors: Germán A. Cipriani; Juan Lupiáñez; Fabiano Botta
articles.abstract: Visual working memory capacity benefits from stimulus meaningfulness, yet it remains unclear whether this advantage also enhances internal attention deployment. This study investigates whether voluntary and involuntary retrospective attention differentially prioritize meaningful (intact real-world objects) versus meaningless (diffeomorphically scrambled) representations. Two hypotheses were tested: (H1) involuntary attention preferentially selects meaningless representations, and (H2) voluntary attentional selection is facilitated by meaningful representations. Across two preregistered experiments, participants performed retro-cueing tasks manipulating cue type (voluntary vs. involuntary), stimulus type (intact vs. scrambled), and cue validity. Accuracy and reaction times were jointly modeled using hierarchical drift-diffusion models to dissociate representational quality (drift rate) from retrieval time (non-decision time). Experiment 1 used hemifield peripheral cues, while Experiment 2 employed single-item peripheral cues to maximize attentional deployment. Results showed that meaningful stimuli yielded higher drift rates, indicating enhanced representational quality. Voluntary attention produced robust retro-cueing effects (RCEs) on both drift rate and non-decision time. Critically, support for H2 emerged in non-decision time in Experiment 2, where the RCE was larger for intact than scrambled representations under voluntary attention. No such difference appeared in drift rate. By contrast, involuntary attention produced RCEs in drift rates but showed no reliable modulation by meaningfulness, providing inconclusive support for H1. These results suggest that although both attentional systems can prioritize internal representations, long-term memory selectively facilitates voluntary retrieval. Importantly, these findings highlight the need to dissociate decisional and non-decisional performance components, as semantic influences on attention emerge selectively in retrieval dynamics rather than in representational quality.

articles.title: Differences in Dishonesty Toward Humans and Artificial Agents: The Role of Agent Type and Pupil Size
articles.authors: Fabiola Diana; Misako Kawahara; Akihiro Tanaka; Shaul Shalvi; Ruud Hortensius; Mariska Kret
articles.abstract: As artificial agents become more involved in social and economic interactions that rely on honest behavior from humans, they are increasingly designed with human-like social cues, such as pupil size, to appear more approachable. Pupil size is subtle, but also a powerful cue that can guide our decisions. Yet, we still know little about how pupil size shapes people’s behavior, especially across cultures where agents and their signals are interpreted and accepted differently. In this study, 275 participants from Europe and Japan played a coin toss game where lying increased financial gain. They interacted with a human confederate, a virtual avatar, or a robot. Agents' pupil sizes were manipulated to appear large or small; for human confederates, this was achieved using custom-designed contact lenses. Our results suggest that cultures differ in how dishonest behavior is directed toward human and artificial agents: European participants were more dishonest toward artificial agents than toward humans, while Japanese participants showed no such differentiation across agent types. Beyond this, pupil size influenced dishonesty specifically towards avatars in culture-specific ways, with European being more honest towards avatar with small pupils compared to large, and the opposite pattern for Japanese participants. Exploratory analyses further revealed cultural differences in mind perception attributions across agent types, alongside overlapping pupillary responses towards agents across cultures. These findings highlight the need to consider cultural context when designing artificial agents that use human-like cues to guide social behavior: what subtly communicates warmth in one culture may signal something else in another.

articles.title: Accumulation, transfer, and maturation in bilingual toddlers’ word learning
articles.authors: Miranda Gómez Díaz; Krista Byers-Heinlein
articles.abstract: Bilingual children typically must learn two labels for each concept they encounter, one in each language, effectively doubling the number of words they acquire relative to monolinguals. While bilinguals typically know fewer words in each language than monolinguals, they do not show the delay that would be expected if learning double the words required double the time. Three mechanisms may explain this: the accumulation of language-specific experience with labels (operationalized as language-adjusted word frequency), transfer across and within languages, and maturational changes in learning efficiency. The present studies investigate the interplay of these factors using computational simulations (Study 1) and models of empirical data from toddlers aged 16-30 months acquiring English and/or French (Study 2). Simulations progressively incorporated language exposure and transfer between translation equivalents, establishing that accumulator models require some degree of transfer to produce realistic bilingual vocabulary trajectories. Empirical models first replicated accumulator models with monolingual data, then extended them to bilinguals, confirming that concept frequency, language exposure, and age all predicted word learning, with age emerging as the strongest predictor and modulating the effects of the other two. Moreover, in bilingual children, concept frequency was a stronger predictor of word production than language-adjusted word frequency. Language exposure also independently predicted word production, suggesting that broader language experience supports acquisition beyond the accumulation of experience with individual labels. These findings indicate that accumulation, transfer (both cross-language and within-language), and maturation interact, enabling bilingual children to learn words from two languages without requiring twice the experience.

articles.title: The Impact of Mood and Affective Priming on Art Evaluation:  A Systematic Literature Review
articles.authors: Corinna Kühnapfel; Julian Kutsche; Joerg Fingerhut
articles.abstract:
Many studies in empirical aesthetics investigate emotional responses to art. Yet the role of affective states that precede an encounter with art remains surprisingly underexamined. This systematic review synthesizes evidence on how affective priming, experimentally induced mood, as well as baseline mood shape the evaluation of visual art. Following PRISMA guidelines, we searched PsycINFO, Scopus, and PubMed (1970–2025), yielding 68 records. Eight studies met inclusion criteria, and seven additional studies were identified via backward citation tracking and supplementary searches, resulting in 15 quantitative studies (N = 1,600).
Across studies, affective influences were categorized as affect-congruent, affect-incongruent, arousal-related, or context-dependent. Overall, preceding affect can modulate core evaluative outcomes such as liking, beauty, interest, and sublimity, but effects are heterogeneous. Several priming paradigms show affect-congruent shifts in liking, whereas other studies report affect-incongruent enhancements (e.g., fear increasing sublimity or negative primes increasing beauty). Induced mood findings further suggest sensitivity to the specific induction method (e.g., clearer evaluation shifts for text-based, self-focused inductions than for film clips). Baseline mood effects appear more conditional and context-dependent, emerging most clearly when contextual information or task demands invite open elaboration. 
We map the evidence onto Forgas’s Affect Infusion Model (AIM), arguing that variability across findings is interpretable in terms of judgment strategies (heuristic vs. substantive vs. motivated processing) and corresponding routes of affect integration. We conclude with methodological priorities and future directions aimed at directly testing mechanisms and embodied pathways through which affect shapes aesthetic evaluation.

articles.title: Eye-Movement Components Underlying Children’s Passage Reading
articles.authors: Charlotte Jeppsen; Leah Zimmermann; Keith Baxelbaum; Bob McMurray
articles.abstract:
A key goal of reading instruction is to develop efficient silent reading. Yet, the cognitive processes that support this development are not fully understood. Eye-movements are commonly used to index many of the cognitive subprocesses that support reading (e.g., word recognition). However, eye-movement control may be a critical independent component. Traditional passage reading eye-movement (PREM) indices, such as first fixation duration and regressions (Rayner, 1997), develop with age, yet we do not yet have a comprehensive picture of how individual differences in reading and reading-related skills in emerging readers link to differences in these PREM indices. This study asked how age, reading abilities, oral language, and efficient lexical access (assessed with the Visual World Paradigm) contribute to variation in children’s PREMs. We analyzed data from 212 English-speaking children (107 male, 105 female; Grade: M = 4.00, SD = 0.76) who silently read passages taken from the Test of Reading Comprehension – 4th Edition (Brown et al., 2009). Commonality analyses revealed that decoding and efficient lexical access accounted for significant variance across multiple PREM indices, while age only uniquely predicted shorter word reading times. A principal component analysis identified three distinct reading dimensions to PREM control—methodical, disorganized, and disengaged, suggesting that aspects of eye-movements may assemble in complex ways to support reading.  These patterns together explained 26% of the variance in reading fluency. This suggests that gains in word recognition efficiency may be critical to the development of efficient reading, alongside general gains in control of eye-movements during reading. 
Keywords: passage reading, eye movements, reading, development, word recognition, visual world paradigm, eye-tracking

articles.title: Toward Scalable Eye-Tracking: Validating the Tobii Nexus Webcam-Based System
articles.authors: Kim Astor; Pär Nyström; Viveka Andersson; Vera Birath; Elsa Söderbaum; Gustaf Gredebäck
articles.abstract: Webcam-based eye tracking offers scalable alternatives to laboratory systems, but its suitability for behavioral research remains unclear. We evaluated Tobii Nexus against a conventional infrared eye tracker (Tobii Pro Nano) in laboratory and out-of-lab settings (N = 101). Nexus achieved 1.64° accuracy and 0.85° precision in the lab, compared to 0.96° and 0.68° for Nano. Performance declined modestly for Nexus in the out-of-lab condition, primarily in precision, while data availability remained high. Accuracy improved toward central screen positions and was largely unaffected by variability in stimuli background brightness. These results indicate that Nexus provides stable and interpretable gaze estimates suitable for many area-of-interest–based paradigms. While infrared systems retain superior spatial precision, webcam-based tracking offers a viable and scalable alternative for a broad range of behavioral applications.

articles.title: You know, like, op- optionality: Grammatical optionality does not trigger discourse markers nor repairs
articles.authors: Thomas Van Hoey; Ruiming Ma; Matt Hunt Gardner; Benedikt Szmrecsanyi
articles.abstract: Grammatical optionality, or intra-speaker variation between functionally similar forms, is often treated as cognitively costly, inefficient, or unstable following from the doctrine of form-function symmetry (e.g., the Principle of No Synonymy). We tested this assumption using 501 manually annotated conversations from SWITCHBOARD, a corpus of spoken American English. The dataset comprised 47,913 turns, 18,294 optionality contexts across 22 English alternations, and 179,408 dysfluencies. Unlike earlier work, we define dysfluency broadly, including not only filled and unfilled pauses but also restarts and repairs, discourse markers, and editing terms. Using linear mixed-effects regression with speaker as a random effect and turn duration, speech rate, and mean word length as controls, we find no evidence that optionality increases dysfluency. On the contrary, optionality is negatively correlated with canonical pauses and with the overall dysfluency measure. The findings show no significant positive effects for the additional dysfluency types. These findings argue against strong form-function symmetry claims and support a probabilistic Principle of Optionality.

articles.title: Patterns of Online Credibility Evaluation Among Fluent and Struggling Readers
articles.authors: Laura Kanniainen; Asko Tolvanen; Eija Räikkönen; Panayiota Kendeou; Carita Kiili
articles.abstract: This study examined online credibility evaluation skills of 728 students: fourth, sixth, eighth, and 10th graders. In a grade-invariant task, students read four online texts and evaluated them regarding author expertise, author benevolence, and quality of evidence. Latent class analysis identified four subgroups of online credibility evaluators: uncritical, distrustful, unsystematic, and emergent. Over two-thirds of students were either uncritical or distrustful evaluators. Students from all grade levels were represented in all latent evaluator subgroups. While students with reading difficulties were also distributed relatively evenly across the evaluator subgroups, mean-level differences in reading fluency were observed between the groups. Both students with and without reading difficulties struggled with credibility evaluation, highlighting the need for support tailored to their needs as evaluators. For uncritical evaluators, instructions could emphasize questioning and critical evaluation strategies, while distrustful evaluators may benefit from guidance on distinguishing credible sources from unreliable ones without resorting to blanket rejection.

articles.title: Limits to intuition in number compositionality:  the case of multiplicative commutativity
articles.authors: Marie Amalric; Elizabeth Spelke; Manuela Piazza
articles.abstract: Commutativity—the principle that operand order does not affect the result of an operation—is a core feature of two foundational operations of arithmetic: addition and multiplication. Recent theories propose that such compositional structures emerge from an innate “language of thought” that enables abstract principles to apply flexibly across symbolic and non-symbolic formats. While children possess early intuitions about additive commutativity, less is known about their grasp of the commutativity of multiplication. Here, we ask whether children’s understanding of the commutative principle of multiplication stems from pre-existing intuitions that the order in which sets are grouped does not matter, or whether it instead emerges from reasoning about structural relations among numerical symbols. We tested 8- to 9-year-old children in a number comparison game, probing their understanding of multiplicative commutativity in both symbolic expressions (3x2 = 2x3) and in non-symbolic sets of grouped dots. On average, children performed better with symbolic expressions than with non-symbolic arrays. Moreover, their performance on the arrays of grouped dots was directly linked to their mastery of commutativity in symbolic expressions, and independent of their intuitive, non-symbolic numerosity estimation skills. To probe children’s developing mastery of the commutativity of multiplication, a subset of the children were given brief training on commutativity and played the same game in a second session, 2 to 14 days later. Children who, in the first session, did not master the commutative principle with symbolic stimuli showed improvement on symbolic trials, while improvement on non-symbolic trials lagged for the entire group. These findings suggest that symbolic learning of the principle of commutativity of multiplication comes first, before children can apply it to concrete contexts. Moreover, they provide evidence for format- and operation-specific limits to children’s mastery of the compositional structure of the natural number system.

articles.title: Partial Metric Invariance in the Latent Actor-Partner Interdependence Model: A Simulation Study Evaluating the Performance of Four Analytical Approaches
articles.authors: Shiyao Wang; Andres Felipe Perez Alonso; Eva Ceulemans
articles.abstract: When modeling interdependence between two individuals using the latent Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (the latent APIM), metric invariance is essential but often only partially achieved in practice. This paper evaluates four analytical approaches under partial metric invariance: sum scoring, full-invariance modeling, partial-invariance modeling, and invariance-ignoring modeling. For the latter two approaches, we further examine the impact of misspecifying noninvariant items. Results show that sum scoring yielded biased estimates, whereas the other three approaches produced accurate standardized estimates. Invariance-ignoring modeling was most robust to misspecification regarding unstandardized estimates. These findings offer practical guidance for researchers modeling dyadic interdependence with latent constructs.

articles.title: A Glance in the Cognitive Mirror: Validation of an Interpretation Bias Task  for Body Dissatisfaction
articles.authors: Marielaura Hörning-Suermann; Marcella Lydia Woud; Mira A. Preis
articles.abstract: Interpretation biases (IBs) are thought to play a central role in body dissatisfaction (BD) and eating disorder (ED) psychopathology, yet validated assessment tools are limited. This study validated a newly developed German scenario based task (Body Dissatisfaction Interpretation Task; BDIT) designed to assess IBs in the context of BD. An online sample of cisgender women completed the BDIT and self report measures at two time points separated by 14 days (T1: N = 244; T2: N = 176). The task comprises 24 ambiguous everyday scenarios (distributed across three booklets) followed by BD related, negative non BD-related, and neutral interpretations. The BDIT demonstrated excellent internal consistency (α = .85-.96), high test-retest reliability (ICC = .82-.91), and strong agreement across the three booklets (ICC = .83-.89). Convergent validity was supported by strong associations with BD and ED symptoms (r = .69-.79). Divergent validity was mixed, with weak correlations for unrelated constructs (alcohol consumption; spider anxiety; r = .10-.25), but moderate associations with anxiety‑related measures (agoraphobic cognitions; social anxiety, r = .44-.60), consistent with models conceptualizing IBs as partly transdiagnostic but showing graded specificity. The BDIT explained unique variance in BD beyond age, BMI, and ED symptoms, and showed a small, non-significant prospective effect over 14 days (β = .10, p = .092). These findings provide preliminary support for the BDIT as a reliable, valid, and easily implementable tool for assessing (changes in) IB in the context of BD, with potential applications in intervention studies evaluating cognitive change.

articles.title: Short words are more likely to refer to multiple meanings across 192 language families
articles.authors: Alexey Koshevoy; Marie Hallo; Rowan Hall Maudslay; Olivier Morin
articles.abstract: This study tested, for the first time on a diverse cross-linguistic dataset, the existence of a negative correlation between a word’s length and its semantic ambiguity. This correlation can be predicted on various grounds: as a way to make communication more efficient, as a mechanical consequence of the greater availability of short wordforms, or as a corollary of Zipf’s laws of abbreviation and meaning, which state that frequent words are shorter and have more meanings. We tested the correlation between word length and number of meanings in a broad range of languages, our biggest analysis studying 633,308 wordforms in 1,952 languages representing 192 families. We operationalise word ambiguity in three ways: as whether or not a word colexifies several meanings, using Lexibank data, as the number of synsets in Wordnet, and using BERT-derived predictions of the degree of ambiguity of a word. For all three measures of ambiguity, we evidence a robust correlation between a word’s brevity and ambiguity. The correlation remains substantial when controlling for word frequency, suggesting that it is more than a by-product of the fact that frequent words tend to be both shorter and more ambiguous.

articles.title: Goals shape dynamics of attention and selection for value-based decision-making
articles.authors: Romy Frömer; Chih-Chung Ting; Amitai Shenhav; Sebastian Gluth
articles.abstract: Humans can flexibly adjust how they make decisions to arbitrary goals. However, most theories in decision-making focus on predicting one specific choice type (i.e., choosing the best option). Here, we link decision-making and cognitive-control research to test a theory that accounts for flexible adjustments of choice mechanisms to different goals and demands. Our biologically inspired model specifies how different features translate into evidence for the current goal, and how evidence is mapped onto different output structures. We tested the model in an eye-tracking study in which participants were asked to choose one out of four consumer products or to appraise the entire set, each with respect to positive or negative value. The results confirmed our preregistered hypotheses that response time (RT) should decrease with the overall value of a set of options in choose-best but increase in choose-worst trials. As predicted, this interaction was absent in appraisal RT, which instead exhibited an inverted-U-shaped pattern. Furthermore, the amount of attention devoted to an option was positively related to its value in choose-best, negatively related in choose-worst trials, and unrelated when participants appraised entire sets of products. Time-resolved analyses of eye movements revealed strategic goal-dependent search processes, as attention is increasingly focused on goal-congruent options in choice but remains more uniformly distributed in appraisal. Our findings suggest that cognitive control shapes choice and search dynamics by flexibly adjusting them to current goals and demands.

articles.title: A Digital Shoulder to Cry On: Understanding Why Large Language Models Can Be Effective in Extrinsic Interpersonal Emotion Regulation
articles.authors: Yuhui Chen; Sarah A Walker; Belen Lopez-Perez
articles.abstract: Do large language models (LLMs) provide emotion regulation as effectively as humans? This research investigates when and why LLMs might be preferred as extrinsic interpersonal emotion regulators (EIER) in non-clinical contexts. Across three studies, we systematically compared the regulatory effects and perceived effectiveness of LLM- versus human-generated comforting messages. Study 1a (N = 279) used thematic analysis to show that LLM-generated responses largely mirrored human EIER strategies, with some discrepancies between different LLMs.  Study 1b (N = 390) demonstrated that LLMs sometimes produced stronger regulatory effects, though effects varied by emotional context (no differences for sadness) and outcome (no regulatory effects on fear).  Study 2 (N = 196) again showed LLM regulatory advantages in some scenarios and outcomes but emotional validation (i.e., acknowledgement of the target’s emotional response) did not explain the higher emotional improvement achieved by LLMs. Study 3a (N = 188) identified actionable support (i.e., specific and implementable regulatory tactics) as a feature that enhanced regulatory effectiveness across sources. This was confirmed in Study 3b (N = 180) while controlling for message length. These findings suggest that, when evaluated using prototypical, experimentally generated messages, LLMs can effectively mimic or sometimes surpass human prototypes in EIER. However, advantages seem to be context- and outcome- dependent. Importantly, actionable support emerges as a key component for effective interpersonal emotion regulation across sources. Implications are discussed for theories of interpersonal emotion regulation and AI-human interaction.

articles.title: Metacognition and post-decisional processing in clinical decision-making
articles.authors: Sucharit Katyal; Stephen M Fleming
articles.abstract:
Decision-making does not culminate when a choice is made. Instead, humans continue to evaluate their decisions through post-decisional reflection, and when needed, use new evidence to update the original decision. Post-decisional evaluation enables the formation of confidence in a decision, and detection and of revision errors. These processes belong to a class cognitive processes labelled metacognition—the capacity to monitor, evaluate, and regulate one’s own cognitive processing. Understanding post-decisional dynamics is especially consequential in clinical decision making, where initial decisions are often made under uncertainty and time pressure, and may need to be revised as new information emerges.
In this review, we synthesise theoretical, behavioural, and neuroscientific work on metacognition and post-decisional processing, focusing on evidence accumulation frameworks. We describe how classic decision models, such as drift–diffusion models, can be extended beyond the point of choice to account for confidence, error detection, and changes of mind. We discuss how confidence is dynamically updated by post-decisional information, underpinned by neural signatures of continued evidence accumulation and performance monitoring. We also examine how these processes are shaped by systematic biases, including a confidence-dependent confirmation bias.
Finally, we highlight recent computational approaches that reveal different sources of metacognitive bias, including distortions in post-decisional accumulation and differences in how evidence is interpreted, offering new explanations of individual differences in belief updating. In clinical decision-making, such mechanisms may determine whether practitioners appropriately revise or persist with initial judgments, with important implications for diagnostic accuracy and patient outcomes.

articles.title: Human-AI dialogues can reduce feelings of partisan animosity
articles.authors: Dylan Thurgood; Mohsen Mosleh; Jan G. Voelkel; David Gertler Rand
articles.abstract: Affective polarization – and especially dislike of opposing partisans – is a source of major concern. Consequently, there is great interest in identifying effective approaches for depolarization. Here, we ask whether recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI), namely the development of conversational large language models, can be leveraged to reduce affective polarization. Prior work suggests that such AI-powered chatbots can be powerful tools for changing factual beliefs. However, it remains unclear whether dialogues with an AI chatbot can reduce feelings of animosity towards members of one’s political outgroup. To answer this question, we assign N = 3,874 US-based participants to one of three treatments in which an AI chatbot tries to reduce affective polarization, to a control where they have an irrelevant dialogue with the AI, or to a benchmark condition consisting of the top-performing non-AI intervention identified in a recent mega-study. We find that all of the human-AI dialogue treatments significantly reduce animosity towards opposing partisans and discomfort with having opposing partisans as neighbors or close friends, with the most effective AI treatment achieving treatment effects roughly 70% the size of the effect of the top-performing non-AI intervention benchmark. In addition, we find that the human-AI dialogues increase the likelihood that individuals reciprocate social ties with hypothetical social media profiles associated with their political outgroup by approximately five percentage points, performing at least as well as the non-AI intervention benchmark. We find that supplying the AI chatbot with specific facts about the outgroup increases effectiveness. These findings indicate that providing factual information is an important route to depolarization, and that AI chatbots offer an effective approach for reducing affective polarization that has the potential to be deployed at scale online.

articles.title: Developing Frameworks for the Motives and Strategies People Use to Make Others Feel Worse
articles.authors: Carolyn MacCann; Alexander Mitchell; Lana Christina Haj; Lisa E. Kim; Iana Yuen Ting Wong
articles.abstract: How we regulate others’ emotions can have a positive impact (when improving emotions) or negative impact (when worsening emotions) on relationships and emotional wellbeing. The current study aims to develop theoretical frameworks of motives and strategies for making others feel worse. We conducted a content analysis of 90 incidents of attempts to make someone feel worse, drawn from a 2024 online survey of 50 adults from five English-speaking countries. Deductive frameworks were adapted given the collected data. The motives framework includes 11 motives (dominate, retaliate, gain autonomy, make them look worse, make me look better, make them feel worse, make me feel better, achieve my outcome, achieve their outcome, manage their expectations, make them seek truth) categorised by regulation target (self-focus, other-focus) and regulation aim (change power, change appearance, change emotion, change outcome, change thought). The strategies framework grouped eight strategies into four themes ordered from high to low engagement with the target: insulting (aggressing, taunting), fault-finding (shaming, guilting), downplaying (showing off, cautioning) and withdrawing (withholding, ignoring). Motive/strategy contingencies showed distinct patterns of motives for different strategies (e.g., self-focused motives were linked to shaming, other-focused motives to guilting). Motives and strategies differed in their associations with target/regulator closeness, and in their perceived impact on the target’s emotions and target/regulator relationship. The two frameworks facilitate a deeper understanding of how and why people attempt to make others feel worse, illustrating the fundamental mechanisms underlying such behaviors in contexts characterized by conflict and aggression, such as intimate partner violence, bullying and abuse.

articles.title: Consensus-based Checklist for Assessing Scientific Objectivity
articles.authors: Michal Sikorski; Noah N'Djaye Nikolai van Dongen; Rene Bernard; Antonietta Curci; Ana Marusic; s. Clare Stanford; Andrew Stewart; Helene Richter; Tracey Lynn Weissgerber
articles.abstract: Objectivity is widely regarded as crucial for scientific practice, yet it remains poorly understood in scientific methodology and, as a result, has limited impact on research practices. Building on recent developments in the philosophy of science, we have created a new methodological tool, an easy to use checklist, for assessing the objectivity of scientific practice reported in academic publications or grant proposals. Developed through an expert-consensus study, this checklist can be used both to retrospectively evaluate objectivity of completed research and to prospectively guide the design of future experiments.

articles.title: Minds Meet Machines: Trust and Intellectual Humility as Necessary Conditions for AI Persuasion
articles.authors: Devora Newman; Almog Simchon; Ruth Mayo
articles.abstract: Recently, AI models demonstrated a remarkable ability to facilitate opinion change, a notoriously difficult task between human communicators. Here, we focus on the dispositions that characterize message recipients, specifically identifying trust in AI, intellectual humility (IH), and their interplay as key factors in acceptance of counter-attitudinal information. Across two studies with an online British sample (N=294), we replicated findings showing significant attitude change regarding legal immigrants following brief interactions with GPT. Crucially, our findings reveal that opinion change is contingent on higher IH and greater trust in AI, independently and jointly, predicting greater acceptance of counter-attitudinal information. Experimentally capturing the centrality of open-mindedness (IH) and source sensitivity (Trust) highlights how epistemic vigilance constrains AI’s persuasive impact and sheds new light on the persuasion literature by revisiting the role of source credibility. Future research should examine whether these dispositional effects extend to long-term behavioral patterns across broader real-world contexts.

articles.title: How methodological cues shape trust in psychological research and legal judgments: A vignette study
articles.authors: Sera Wiechert; Elise de Groot; Bastiaan T Rutjens; Yoni Pertzov lab; Bruno Verschuere
articles.abstract: Over the past decade, psychology has embraced transparency and reproducibility reforms to address the replication crisis, raising the question of whether these signals help legal decision-makers identify which psychological evidence is sufficiently credible to rely on. This study examines how these reforms influence perceived study credibility and decision-making in a legally relevant setting. We hypothesized that higher methodological quality (larger samples, preregistration, and replications or meta-analyses) would increase perceived credibility of psychological evidence and decrease guilt judgments. We conducted an online vignette experiment including individuals with Doctorates and laypersons, where participants evaluated fictitious legal cases accompanied by manipulated research summaries. Results showed that larger sample sizes consistently enhanced perceived credibility across both groups, while preregistration did not affect credibility. Doctorate participants rated replications and meta-analyses as more credible than primary studies, while laypersons did not differentiate among study designs. Importantly, none of the methodological quality cues influenced guilt judgments. These findings highlight the need for clearer education about research norms to enhance the integration of psychological science in real-world decision-making. Future research should explore more ecologically valid designs and a broader range of methodological indicators to better understand how perceptions of scientific credibility shape applied reasoning.

articles.title: Patterns of Online Credibility Evaluation Among Fluent and Struggling Readers
articles.authors: Laura Kanniainen; Asko Tolvanen; Eija Räikkönen; Panayiota Kendeou; Carita Kiili
articles.abstract: This study examined online credibility evaluation skills of 728 students: fourth, sixth, eighth, and 10th graders. In a grade-invariant task, students read four online texts and evaluated them regarding author expertise, author benevolence, and quality of evidence. Latent class analysis identified four subgroups of online credibility evaluators: uncritical, distrustful, unsystematic, and emergent. Over two-thirds of students were either uncritical or distrustful evaluators. Students from all grade levels were represented in all latent evaluator subgroups. While students with reading difficulties were also distributed relatively evenly across the evaluator subgroups, mean-level differences in reading fluency were observed between the groups. Both students with and without reading difficulties struggled with credibility evaluation, highlighting the need for support tailored to their needs as evaluators. For uncritical evaluators, instructions could emphasize questioning and critical evaluation strategies, while distrustful evaluators may benefit from guidance on distinguishing credible sources from unreliable ones without resorting to blanket rejection.

articles.title: Meta-Awareness of Mind Wandering: Examining Effects of Self-Regulated Learning, Interpolated Testing, and Self-Explanation
articles.authors: Daniel Ebbert; Alrike Claassen; Natasha Wilson; Srecko Joksimovic; Negin Mirriahi; Shane Dawson
articles.abstract:
Background
Mind wandering is a common experience for students that negatively impacts learning outcomes. Research has attempted to mitigate the impact by including interpolated testing in the context of learning from videos. The results of these studies are mixed and indicate that interpolated testing may not have a practical effect on reducing mind wandering.
Objectives
In the present study, we aim to investigate whether writing self-explanations has a stronger effect than interpolated testing on reducing mind wandering and improving learning outcomes. We al-so explore whether self-regulated learning skills are related to meta-awareness of mind wander-ing.
Methods
138 participants were recruited across three groups and presented with the same video to review. The first group was the control group. The participants in the second group answered interpolated tests, and the third group wrote self-explanations at pauses in the video. All participants complet-ed a knowledge test before and after watching the video to compare group learning outcomes. Additionally, participants completed questionnaires measuring their self-regulated learning. Self-caught thought reports were used in anticipation that participants expecting to write self-explanations would engage in metacognitive monitoring and thus become meta-aware.
Results and Conclusions
We found no significant difference between the groups regarding knowledge gain or meta-awareness. Interestingly, higher scores on metacognition and online task strategies subscales of self-regulated learning were associated with fewer thought reports.
