The method is grounded in the philosophical concept of “speech seeking orientation under finitude,” which posits that speech in the context of serious illness not only conveys symptoms, preferences, or beliefs, but also seeks orientation, relational connection, containment, and a livable position within altered life circumstances. The accompanying philosophical manuscript contends that such speech is conveyed not only through semantic content but also through pacing, contour, hesitation, silence, recurrence, breath, and reaction, and that music therapy is uniquely positioned to address these expressive modalities. CATHA translates this insight into a transparent mixed-methods workflow comprising audio processing, waveform inspection, therapist-defined segmentation, acoustic feature extraction, score-like contour rendering, and uncertainty marking. The method is not intended as an emotion-recognition system, diagnostic tool, or predictor of clinical outcomes. Rather, CATHA serves as a methodological prototype for integrating transcript, vocal form, therapist interpretation, and reproducible research artefacts. This method's paper outlines the conceptual foundation, implementation logic, intended application in music therapy, validation strategy, and ethical considerations of CATHA. CATHA is available as an R/Shiny prototype (https://github.com/sgjerga/CATHA) and as an online application (https://sgjerga-catha.share.connect.posit.cloud/).

articles.title: From Wounded Healers to Commercialized Caregivers: A Scoping Review of Mental Health Content Creators on Social Media
articles.authors: Nicole Bizzotto; Qianfeng Lu; Srusti Bhalerao; Krysten Stein; Amanda Yarnell; Tobias Dienlin; Laura Marciano
articles.abstract: An increasing number of content creators, including clinicians and people with lived experiences, are sharing mental health–related content on social media. This review maps the empirical literature on mental health content creators, with the aim of synthesizing evidence on motivations for content creation and engagement, communicative practices, and associated outcomes for both creators and their audiences. In addition, it advances a working definition of mental health content creators that integrates and extends definitions identified in the literature. The search across nine databases identified 59 peer-reviewed studies published between 2018 and 2025, examining a wide range of platforms, most commonly YouTube (35.6%), TikTok (23.7%), and Instagram (20.3%). Most studies focused on content creators with lived experience of mental disorders (81.4%), whereas only a small minority examined those with professional expertise (8.5%). Beyond mental health conditions in general terms (32.2%), the most frequently shared topics were neurodevelopmental disorders (15.3%), feeding and eating disorders (13.6%), and depressive disorders (10.2%). Communicative practices were investigated across four domains: psychoeducation (addressed in 66.1% of studies), service promotion (5.1%), self-disclosure (47.5% reported positive framings; 47.5% reported negative framings), and advocacy (30.5%). The included studies reported a wide range of motivations for content creation, spanning from altruistic intentions to help others to self-validation. Motivations underlying audience engagement remained comparatively underexplored. Across studies, findings reveal persistent tensions between platformed care and commerce, highlighting how platform logics such as algorithmic visibility shape disclosure practices and audience engagement. Studies most frequently reported positive effects for audiences (47.5%), such as increases in mental health literacy. Fewer identified negative effects (13.6%), including phenomena such as mass social media–induced illness, while 30.5% identified mixed effects, reporting both beneficial and adverse impacts within the same study. For content creators, documented outcomes were also heterogeneous, and included not only a sense of community, validation, and monetization opportunities but also psychological costs such as risks of burnout.

articles.title: Affective Measures for Italian (AMI): Valence, Arousal and Dominance Norms for 13,495 Italian Nouns, Adjective and Verbs
articles.authors: Marco A. Petilli; Alessandra Vergallito; Marco Marelli; Simona Amenta
articles.abstract:
Words affective properties influence a broad range of cognitive processes and should be carefully considered when selecting verbal stimuli for experimental tasks. Although large-scale affective norm databases are available in several languages, Italian still lacks such an extensive resource. This limitation is relevant given the rich inflectional structure of Italian, which offers unique opportunities to investigate how morpho-syntactic variations may shape words affective processing.
To address this gap, we present a large-scale database collecting ratings of valence, arousal, and dominance for 13,495 Italian words, including three major parts of speech (PoS): nouns, adjectives, and verbs. Singular forms were included for nouns; masculine and feminine singular forms for adjectives; and, for verbs, infinitives together with first- and third-person singular present-tense forms of the indicative mood. 
Analyses showed that the distribution patterns of Italian affective ratings are largely consistent with those reported in previous affective norming studies. The relationship among the affective dimensions displayed the typical structure observed in the literature: valence and dominance showed a strong positive linear association, whereas arousal displayed significant quadratic U-shaped relationships with both valence and dominance, highlighting higher activation for words receiving extreme ratings in valence and dominance. These patterns were substantially replicated across grammatical classes.
The present dataset expands previous affective resources available for Italian and provides a reliable tool for psycholinguistic, cognitive, and cross-linguistic investigations. Crucially, the inclusion of multiple PoS and morphological forms makes this dataset particularly suitable for exploring how lexical and grammatical properties contribute to affective word processing.

articles.title: 404: Personal Data Not Found — Efficacy of Anonymous Internet-Based Self-Help for Social Anxiety in a Partially Randomized Patient Preference Trial
articles.authors: Stefanie Arnold; Michael Vogt; Dajana Šipka; Johanna Boettcher; Friederike Fenski; Malte Elson; Thomas Berger
articles.abstract:
Background:
Internet-based interventions can improve access to evidence-based treatments. However, concerns about privacy and data security remain barriers to their adoption. Designing interventions that allow anonymous use may reduce these barriers, but whether anonymity affects treatment efficacy remains unclear.
Objective:
This study compared clinical outcomes of an anonymous internet-based self-help intervention for social anxiety to a conventional non-anonymous version and investigated whether participants’ preferences regarding anonymity influenced treatment outcomes.
Methods:
In this partially randomized patient preference trial, 452 adults with clinically relevant social anxiety symptoms were assigned by randomization or preference to an anonymous or non-anonymous 8-week unguided cognitive behavioral self-help program. Assessments were conducted at baseline, post-treatment, and 24-week follow-up. The primary outcome was social anxiety severity. Mixed-effects models were used to evaluate changes over time and differences between program versions.
Results
Participants showed significant reductions in social anxiety symptoms over time with medium to large within-group effect sizes (d = -0.81 to -0.62). No significant superiority of the non-anonymous version was observed in the randomized or preference arms. Secondary outcomes including depressive symptoms, quality of life, and internalized stigma also improved. Effects were maintained at follow-up and did not differ by allocation method. Further exploratory analyses did not provide robust evidence of outcome differences by preference match.
Discussion
The anonymous intervention was not outperformed by the conventional non-anonymous program. These findings provide preliminary evidence that anonymity need not compromise intervention efficacy and strong privacy protections may help address privacy-related barriers.

articles.title: Serial Dependence Scales with Action-binding Depth in Duration Perception
articles.authors: JiaoWu; Aylin Gündogdu; Halit Can Akgün; Ceren Songur; Zhuanghua Shi
articles.abstract: Serial dependence biases perception toward recent history, yet it often appears only when observers respond actively. The critical issue, however, is whether the effect depends on motor output itself or on how strongly stimulus features are built into the response plan. We tested this by comparing active duration reproduction with passive following, where observers simply tracked stimulus onset and offset with a sustained keypress. Twenty-four participants completed both tasks with physically matched sensory input, equating stimulus properties and motor execution, while the degree of coupling between duration and action varied. The sequential bias was strongest for reproduction-after-reproduction trials, weaker for reproduction-after-following trials, and minimal when the current trial required following. Passive following still showed central tendency, with offset latency revealing implicit duration encoding, but it generated little attraction across trials. These findings point to action-binding depth, rather than motor output alone, as the key driver of serial dependence in time perception.

articles.title: Pseudo-Factor Analysis with Item Intercepts
articles.authors: Nigel Guenole
articles.abstract:
We extend the pseudo-factor analysis method by adding a mean
structure to obtain pseudo-intercepts.We do so by substituting the item
response mean vector based on human responses with a scalar vector
obtained by projecting item embeddings via dot products onto semantic
intensity axes capturing the difference between low and high scale
anchors. Across six scales measuring the trait domains of the DSM-5
alternative model for personality disorders in occupational settings,
the mean correlation between empirical and pseudo-intercepts was
r = .54, while the mean correlation between empirical and pseudointercepts
obtained via deliberate projection onto randomly incorrect
semantic axes was r = −.35. The inclusion of embedding-based
means proxies in pseudo-factor analysis can potentially enhance scale
development and allow mean and covariance structure modeling without
empirical data. This may open the possibility of examining relative
item locations, multiple-group pseudo-modeling, and measurement invariance
analyses. Topics such as systematic methods to identify semantic
anchors and risks including ‘anchor hacking’ are discussed.

articles.title: Feedback anticipation and integration in risk preferences from childhood to elderhood
articles.authors: Antonios Nasioulas; Oana Stanciu; Maël Lebreton; Stefano Palminteri; Azzurra Ruggeri
articles.abstract: Risk preferences are shaped by outcome anticipation and feedback integration. Understanding how these processes evolve across the human lifespan is essential for theoretical models of decision-making, for applications in education, and behavioral public policy aimed at protecting against age-related vulnerabilities. This study examines the lifespan development (N = 658; ages 8 to 78) of decision-making under risk through the lens of these two constitutive components, using a behavioral paradigm merging descriptive and experiential information. Our findings replicate the established pattern of a peak of risk-taking during adolescence, followed by a steady decline with aging. Moreover, our results show that while the influence of outcome anticipation matures gradually during childhood and adolescence, the propensity to learn from feedback diminishes progressively with age. These results suggest that while adults may rely on more sophisticated internal models of probability, these models may paradoxically support faulty reasoning, potentially contributing to suboptimal choices.

articles.title: Making Food VAT Reform Tangible: Concrete Price Information Increases Public Acceptance
articles.authors: Hellen Temme; Cornelia Betsch; Philipp Sprengholz
articles.abstract: Increasing the value-added tax (VAT) on animal- and reducing it on plant-based foods can reduce greenhouse gas emissions and diet-related disease burden by shifting consumption toward more sustainable dietary patterns. Yet, public acceptance remains low. Two pre-registered online experiments in Germany examined whether acceptance is linked to cost overestimation and whether communicating accurate price information increases public support. In Study 1 (N = 161), participants submitted a personal grocery receipt and were randomly assigned to one of three VAT scenarios: an increase on animal-based products (7% to 19%), a reduction on plant-based products (7% to 0%), or both. Policy acceptance was measured before and after participants estimated the price change of their receipt and received feedback on the actual change. Although participants systematically misestimated costs, initial acceptance (β = .92, p < .001) rather than misestimation (β = .03, p = .329) was the dominant predictor of subsequent acceptance. When given the opportunity to eliminate products from their receipt after receiving feedback on the actual change, participants predominantly removed meat and dairy. Study 2 (N = 299) focused on the VAT combination policy using a standardized €30 receipt. Participants either estimated costs before receiving feedback, received accurate price information only, or received only a policy description. Acceptance in the estimation (d = .54) and information (d = .33) conditions significantly increased compared to the control condition, while misestimation showed no significant relationship with acceptance. These findings suggest that expressing price changes in Euros rather than VAT percentages represents a simple, scalable strategy for increasing public support for sustainable food taxation.

articles.title: Memorability as Emergent Property: How Representational Structure Creates Apparent Intrinsic Effects
articles.authors: Dyllan Simpson; John Wixted; Timothy F. Brady
articles.abstract: Some pictures, videos, faces, voices, and words are reliably remembered better than others, a phenomenon known as memorability. This has been widely interpreted as reflecting a stable, intrinsic property of the stimuli themselves. Here we argue that this consistency is better explained by the relational structure of the stimulus sets used to measure memorability, rather than as an intrinsic property of the items. Using simulations with a global matching model of recognition memory, we show that apparent intrinsic memorability emerges naturally from distinctiveness — how dissimilar an item is from others in a given set of encoded images — without any stimulus-intrinsic mechanism. In behavioral experiments, we demonstrate that manipulating the similarity structure of image sets can invert memorability rankings, making previously memorable images forgettable and vice versa. We also show that features previously identified as intrinsically memorable (such as “body-part related”) are the most representationally distinctive features in standard databases, explaining their memorability. To address these large variations in distinctiveness we created a new image set that reduced variability in representational distinctiveness, and found it significantly diminishes all feature-memorability relationships. These findings suggest that memorability is not a fixed attribute of items themselves, but an emergent property of which items are distinctive within the context, with implications for how we attribute stable psychological properties to stimuli more broadly.

articles.title: Evaluating User Perspective on a Mindfulness Based- Intervention for Improving Risk and Protective Factors of Disordered Eating: A Person- Based Approach.
articles.authors: Vanshika Drolia
articles.abstract:
Objective: This study aimed to evaluate a brief  digital mindfulness intervention designed to target body image and eating disorder risk factors among young adults aged 16-34, as part of a wider mixed methods research taking a person-based approach during the development of a digital mindfulness-based intervention.
Method: A person-based approach was used involving think-aloud interviews and semi-structured interviews with 10 participants (5 Male, 5 Female) (mean age=26.5, SD=5.68)  without current eating disorders. Reflexive thematic analysis was conducted following Braun and Clarke's (2006) six-phase approach.
Results: Three main themes emerged - usability, acceptability, and feasibility of the tailored mindfulness program. Participants found the content clear, simple, coherent and engaging. They reported increased self-awareness and more adaptive thought patterns, indicating acceptability. All participants intended to continue practicing the techniques, reflecting feasibility. However, participants also mentioned practical challenges in maintaining focus during the mindfulness practices. The table of changes created after the first set of interviews allowed incorporation of modifications to address issues like reducing long pauses and adding more guidance during exercises based on initial user feedback.
Discussion: While an expanded trial is warranted, findings provide initial evidence that accessible, tailored digital mindfulness tools can successfully engage youth and cultivate protective skills relevant to body image and disordered eating prevention. Further research into optimized blended digital mindfulness interventions is needed as an engaging early prevention approach for escalating mental health risks among young people. The digital format enables scalable delivery on platforms youth already access.
Keywords: digital mindfulness-based intervention, person- based approach, disordered eating, young adults, prevention.

articles.title: The Cannabis Research Image Database (CRESIDA): a standardized and validated image set for studying cannabis cue-reactivity
articles.authors: Janna Cousijn; Emese Kroon; Chao Suo; Tom Freeman; Chandni Hindocha; Marianna Quinones-Valera; Valentina Lorenzetti
articles.abstract: Background and Aims: Cannabis cue-reactivity paradigms are instrumental in studying the behavioral and neurocognitive mechanisms of cannabis use and cannabis use disorders. However, image sets used for cannabis cue-reactivity paradigms vary between studies, and the lack of reliability and validity assessment hinders the quality of evidence they generate. The main aim of this study was to create a novel, open access, standardized, and representative database of cannabis use-related images including control images matched by resolution, luminosity, and complexity: The Cannabis Research Image Database (CRESIDA). The secondary aim was to examine whether subjective cannabis cue-induced craving was associated with cannabis‑use severity and whether this relationship was moderated by image type. As an illustrative example of how our open data can be used and how sample characteristics can shape cue‑reactivity, we also explored the role of cannabis–tobacco mixing by comparing cannabis cue-induced cannabis and tobacco craving between individuals who did and did not mix the substances. Design: An online survey was administered to participants recruited via online platforms, community advertisement, and snowballing. Setting: USA, the Netherlands, and Australia. Participants/Cases: 689 participants who consumed cannabis monthly-to-daily (385 men, 298 women, 6 other) were recruited between January 2022 and May 2024. Measurements: Out of 93 cannabis images and 93 matched neutral images, participants each rated 31 image pairs for cannabis craving (the primary outcome), arousal, valence, and tobacco craving. Participants were characterized for socio-demographic data, level of cannabis use and related problems, and mixing cannabis and tobacco. A subset of 78 images was selected for further analysis based on cannabis craving results. Image ratings were evaluated for internal consistency (). Furthermore, we examined the association between cannabis cravings and cannabis use characteristics, and explored if cannabis craving ratings were affected by image type (i.e., product, paraphernalia, and actions), and by using cannabis alone vs. mixing cannabis and tobacco.  Findings. The database showed excellent reliability ( = .995-.965). Cannabis craving, valence, and arousal discriminated cannabis and control images. More cannabis use days (β = 0.162, p < 0.001) and cannabis-related problems (β = 0.268, p < 0.001) were significantly associated with higher image-related cannabis craving. Mixing cannabis with tobacco, compared to using cannabis alone, was associated with the presence of tobacco craving in relation to cannabis images, and with greater cannabis craving in relation to cannabis images (β = -0.457, p < 0.001).  Conclusions: Images part of the open access CRESIDA (https://osf.io/dc9nz/) are reliable and valid for the scientific study of cue-reactivity internationally, providing a broad range of free to use cannabis and control images.

articles.title: Towards precision psychotherapy: Leveraging session-level data to predict session-by-session treatment outcomes
articles.authors: Felix Strakeljahn; Tania Marie Lincoln; Niklas Jacobs; Celine Brandt; Kevin Hilbert; Katrin Bahlinger; Inga Frantz; Björn Schlier
articles.abstract:
Theoretical Background: Clinical support tools can potentially enhance cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) by providing session-by-session treatment recommendations. To enable such tools, prediction models are needed that can accurately track therapy outcomes based on assessable session data. This study developed models that predict session-by-session mental health levels and identified variables that influence these predictions.
Methods: We analyzed data from a monocentric, naturalistic therapy-process study of outpatients with affective, anxiety, and psychotic disorders (n=327) participating in up to 24 weekly sessions of CBT in routine care in Germany. Within a nested cross-validation approach, we used mixed-effect random forest models to predict session-by-session scores on a brief self-report measure of mental health (i.e., symptoms, mood, distress, functioning) assessed in the next session. Included predictors were baseline psychopathology, type of CBT interventions conducted in the prior session, and patient- and therapist-reported mechanisms of change (e.g., cognitive change, self-understanding, coping) assessed in the prior session. Variable importance was examined using SHAP values.
Results: The mixed-effects random forest predicting next-session outcome achieved an R²=0.23 across the test folds. The most influential predictors were baseline psychopathology and prior session self-reported behavioral mechanisms of change, i.e., engagement in meaningful/positive activities and coping.
Conclusion: Session-by-session prediction of mental health outcomes using routine outcome monitoring data yielded promising predictive accuracy. Self-reported mechanisms of change are particularly valuable for predicting session-by-session mental health levels. These findings may suggest that activating behavioral mechanisms of change in therapy may have a stronger impact on short-term mental health than activating cognitive mechanisms of change.

articles.title: Within-person prospective associations between youth psychopathology and white matter development and the moderating role of parental mental health
articles.authors: Valerie Karl; Dani Beck; Lorenza Dall'Aglio; Niamh MacSweeney; Lucinda Sisk; Bailey Holt-Gosselin; Taylor Jay Keding; Lars T. Westlye; Tilmann von Soest; Dylan G. Gee
articles.abstract:
Background: Adolescence is a developmental period involving ongoing white matter (WM) maturation and rising risk for psychopathology. This risk is particularly elevated among youth whose parents have mental health problems. 
Methods: Leveraging longitudinal data from three waves of the community-based Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (N = 6,581, Nobservations = 16, 271, mean ageTP 1 = 10.2, mean ageTP 2 = 12.0 , mean ageTP 3 = 14.2), we employed random intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPMs) to disentangle stable between-person differences from time-varying within-person changes. We examined prospective and bidirectional associations between youth general psychopathology and diffusion metrics, including fractional anisotropy (FA) and mean diffusivity (MD) in the uncinate fasciculus (UF), superior longitudinal fasciculus (SLF), and corpus callosum (CC). We also tested whether parental psychopathology moderated these associations. 
Results: Preregistered analyses showed no significant between-person or within-person associations between parent-reported youth psychopathology and WM microstructure, nor evidence of moderation by parental psychopathology. In exploratory analyses using youth self-reports, SLF and CC FA were negatively associated with psychopathology at the between-person level, but positively associated at the within-person level during early adolescence. Additionally, SLF and CC MD were negatively associated with self-reported psychopathology on the within-person level during early adolescence, with this association persisting into mid-adolescence for the CC. Parental psychopathology moderated the within-person association between CC MD and self-reported youth psychopathology.
Conclusion: Overall, the findings did not support hypothesized prospective and bidirectional associations between parent-reported youth psychopathology and WM microstructure, nor moderation by parental psychopathology. Instead, our findings highlight limitations of tract-specific explanations for general psychopathology in at-risk youth, informant discrepancies, and the importance of separating between- and within-person effects.

articles.title: Orchestrating neuroimaging data processing using the 'Snakemake' workflow manager
articles.authors: Damien John Mannion; Maria del Mar Quiroga; Jacob M. Paul; Marta Garrido
articles.abstract: The processing of neuroimaging data typically involves a complicated set of operations, which often require different software packages and have intensive computational and storage demands. Although there are many options available for the neuroimaging researcher to establish their preferred set of processing operations, there is relatively little guidance on how to orchestrate such operations within a coherent data processing workflow. Here, we describe the workflow manager 'Snakemake' and introduce its conceptual foundations by referencing an example implementation of a simplified neuroimaging data processing workflow. Snakemake allows researchers to use a Python-based markup language to describe how input files are transformed into output files. Snakemake then uses these rules to schedule and execute the computational jobs that are required to generate the output files, without unnecessary recreation of existing output files. This allows the complete processing workflow to be executed with a single command and creates workflows that are interpretable, efficient, and reproducible.

articles.title: Bilingualism is Associated with Executive Function in Children With Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review and Bayesian Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Xuyi Wang; Jiayan Xie; Ruozhen Hu; Hongwei Ding; Yang Zhang
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Families and clinicians often express concern that bilingual exposure may place additional communicative or cognitive demands on children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD), potentially hindering development. In contrast, researchers generally report no negative effects, though it remains unclear whether bilingualism may confer cognitive benefits. We conducted a systematic review and Bayesian meta-analysis in order to evaluate the association between bilingualism and executive function (EF) in children with ASD.
Methods: We systematically searched five electronic databases and identified 11 studies comparing bilingual and monolingual children with ASD on EF measures (e.g., working memory, inhibitory control, cognitive flexibility). Across studies, 44 effect sizes and 809 participants were included. We implemented a multilevel Bayesian random-effects meta-analysis to estimate the overall association between bilingualism and EF.
Results: We found a small-to-moderate positive association between bilingualism and EF enhancement (Hedges’ g = 0.43, 95% CrI = [0.16, 0.71]). Importantly, the measured EF subdomain and EF measure type moderated this effect, whereas age and gender did not. Generalizability is limited by the predominance of Western, Indo-European samples (10/11 studies) and by heterogeneity across studies.
Conclusion: Contrary to the concerns about cognitive burden, we found limited pooled evidence for small-to-modest EF advantages, which varies across EF measures. Theoretically, our findings are consistent with accounts linking bilingual experience to EF processes. Clinically, maintaining bilingual environments for children with ASD can be beneficial with positive cognitive effects. To address issues of heterogeneity and limited linguistic and cultural diversity, future research should adopt standardized EF measures and clearer definitions of bilingualism using a longitudinal design to inform practice.

articles.title: Toward an Integrative Perspective on Affect in Intergroup Conflict
articles.authors: Adva Gruenwald; Eran Halperin
articles.abstract: Affect is increasingly recognized as a key driver of attitudes and behavior in intergroup conflict. Over the past two decades, research on discrete emotions has flourished, demonstrating how emotions shape responses to conflict-related events, underscoring attitudes and behaviors that either fuel conflict escalation, or contribute to its mitigation and resolution. Other affective processes, such as feelings, moods, and motivations, also play important roles in intergroup conflict, though they have received less scholarly attention and have been studied in a more fragmented manner. At present, there is no comprehensive framework of the role of affect in intergroup conflict. This chapter addresses this gap by proposing an integrative model of different affective processes – including, but not limited to emotion, as shaping attitudes and behaviors in intergroup conflicts. The chapter concludes by outlining directions for future research, emphasizing the need to examine affective processes as interconnected with one another and with non-affective factors.

articles.title: Appraisal-driven Altruism: Examining the Paradoxical Role of Perceived Importance of Helping Others
articles.authors: Teerawat Monnor; Kerstin Preuschoff; Giuseppe Ugazio
articles.abstract: Prosocial action often arises from empathizing with another person’s aversive experience. Yet this empathic reactivity can also be taxing, sometimes reducing the likelihood of helping or impairing action performance. These conflicting influences have been described through the distinction between compassion and personal distress, which represent the promoting and demoting effects of such empathic experiences. Although this framework has generated valuable findings and has been applied in programs designed to cultivate compassion and a prosocial mindset, studies have revealed additional nuances. For example, induced stress may promote action by increasing the sense of urgency. Therefore, another framework is needed to account for the flexible influences of empathic experience. In this study, we aim to contribute to addressing this gap by proposing an appraisal-driven account of prosocial action. Specifically, we demonstrate that perceived importance (PI) can largely account for how prosocial action unfolds and that active regulation is necessary to explain behavioral adaptation arising from prosocial motivation. To examine this perception-to-action mechanism, we adopted a hybrid approach combining hypothesis validation with explanatory analyses. Participants (N = 53) completed a redesigned computer-based effort task in which they attempted to rescue a partner from potential threats, including electric stimulation and monetary loss. Self-report, behavioral performance, and psychological scale data were collected. Results showed that PI increased affective stress and impaired performance, yet performance improved in high-stakes conditions despite elevated stress, distinctly in action initiation. Together, these findings support the appraisal-driven account and highlight its potential to explain the affective dynamics underlying prosocial behavior.

articles.title: Barriers and facilitators of data-based decision-making in Australian sustained nurse home visiting programs: A qualitative study
articles.authors: Ruth Beatson; Jerry Luo; Karen Villanueva; Carly Molloy; Amanda Wood; Lynne Millar; Anna Price; Olivia Hilton; Sharon Goldfeld; Caitlin Macmillan
articles.abstract:
Aims: Sustained Nurse Home Visiting (SNHV) is a widely-implemented model of care that seeks to ameliorate childhood inequities arising from socioeconomic disadvantage. ‘Lead indicators’ are timely, local, accurate, and actionable data that enable monitoring of high-quality, equitable services. We aimed to explore barriers and facilitators of data utilisation in SNHV, to inform how lead indicators can be used to strengthen service delivery and continuous improvement efforts. 
Design: A qualitative study utilising semi-structured interviews and thematic analysis using prespecified codes.
Methods: Interviews were conducted with a purposive sample of seven Australian SNHV staff in management and administrative roles. Analysis was guided by the Theoretical Domains Framework and the Capability, Opportunity and Motivation Model of Behaviour Change.
Results: Participants identified data utilisation barriers and facilitators at the individual- and system-level. These related to: Capability (e.g., knowledge and skills), Opportunity (e.g., workforce capacity, data visualisation and translation support), and Motivation (e.g., attitudes and workplace culture).
Conclusion: Solutions that simultaneously increase capability, opportunity, and motivation are needed to increase effective data utilisation in SNHV delivery.
Implications for the profession: The SNHV workforce needs professional development in effective data utilisation skills. This could be supported by a nationally consistent policy framework to define a common set of lead indicators, and better data availability and access.
Impact: Overcoming barriers and harnessing facilitators to utilise data such as lead indicators will support the SNHV workforce to improve service delivery.

articles.title: Discovering and transmitting abstract knowledge over generations
articles.authors: Ben Prystawski; Michael C. Frank; Noah D. Goodman
articles.abstract: The complexity of human culture depends on people's ability to discover and transmit abstract knowledge. Studying this ability is crucial to understanding humans' distinctive place among species, but current experimental paradigms focus on the cultural transmission of specific, concrete facts rather than generalizable abstract knowledge. In this paper, we develop a crafting game paradigm to study how people discover abstract knowledge and transmit it via language. We compared individuals playing this game for 40 rounds to chains of four participants playing for 10 rounds each and passing messages to each other sequentially. The individuals performed significantly better over rounds, but the chains did not. Through simulations with language model agents and a follow-up experiment, we find substantial variation in the helpfulness of participants' messages, which may explain the lack of consistent improvement in chains. The ability to learn selectively from the good messages may be essential for improvement over generations.

articles.title: More like Guidelines than Hard Rules: Bilinguals Accept Lexical Overlap Despite Using Mutual Exclusivity
articles.authors: Ming Yean Sia; Julien Mayor
articles.abstract: Mutual exclusivity (ME), the tendency that young children have to select a novel object upon hearing a novel label, gives children a basis to select a referent for an unknown label. However, this strategy may not be relevant to bilinguals who learn multiple labels for most objects. Although previous research showed that bilinguals are better than monolinguals in accepting second labels for familiar objects, these studies explicitly labelled the familiar objects with a novel name, leaving open the question of whether children implicitly accept lexical overlap in ambiguous naming situation. We investigated to what extent monolingual and bilingual children associate a novel label with a familiar, name-known object when a novel object is present using a tablet-based referent selection task. Subsequently, we examined whether children retained the novel word-object association as well as whether they formed associations between the novel labels and the familiar, name-known objects. We tested 123 monolingual and 131 bilingual children between 3 and 10 years of age. We found that monolinguals and bilinguals relied on ME but bilinguals to a lesser extent than monolinguals. Monolinguals retained novel word-object associations but not the associations between novel words and familiar objects, while bilinguals retained the associations between novel words and familiar objects but not novel word-object associations. Our study extends the proposition that bilinguals are open to lexical overlap even in ambiguous referent selection situations. Our findings thus indicate that the influence of language experience on children's word-learning strategies persists throughout the first decade of life.

articles.title: Familiar voices attenuate the effects of cognitive load during competing speech
articles.authors: Manda Fischer; Ingrid Johnsrude
articles.abstract: When masked by speech, utterances spoken by a familiar voice are more intelligible than those in a novel voice (the “familiar-voice benefit”), and may also be less cognitively demanding to attend to. If so, a concurrent task should disrupt speech perception less when the attended voice is familiar than when it is novel. Participants (N=30) listened to masked sentences in familiar or novel voices, while tracking four moving dots on a screen (dual task) or ignoring the visual display (single task), and reported target words after each trial. Word report was highest when the target was familiar, lowest when the masker was familiar, and intermediate when both were novel. The dual task reduced intelligibility more for novel than for familiar voices but did not increase the familiar-voice benefit. Familiar voices appear to bias attention and require fewer cognitive resources to process.

articles.title: An AI-Powered Digital Mental Health Platform Reduces Depression and Anxiety Symptoms: A Randomized Controlled Trial
articles.authors: Julie Youko Anne Cachia; Xuan Zhao; Wenxi Pu; Chi Liao; merel Keijsers; Rebecca Fortgang
articles.abstract:
Importance: Depression and anxiety are highly prevalent and disabling, yet most affected individuals receive inadequate or no care. Purpose-built generative AI platforms may offer scalable first-line mental health support, though large-trial evidence remains limited.
Objective: To evaluate whether Flourish, an AI-powered mental health platform, reduces depression and anxiety symptoms over six weeks.
Design, Setting, and Participants: This preregistered randomized clinical trial included 1,137 college students randomized to Flourish or care-as-usual. Participants were recruited without symptom-based screening and assessed at baseline and weeks 2, 4, and 6.
Interventions: Flourish, an AI-powered platform delivering CBT-, DBT-, ACT-, positive psychology–, and motivational interviewing–informed support through an AI well-being coach, Sunnie, vs care-as-usual.
Main Outcomes and Measures: Depression and anxiety symptoms, assessed using the PHQ-2 and GAD-2.
Results: Flourish produced more favorable symptom trajectories than control for depression (condition × time: β = −0.043; p = .006) and anxiety (β = −0.058; p = .001). Consistent with Flourish’s intended role as a first-line support tool, favorable patterns were observed across the baseline symptom spectrum, with less symptom worsening among participants with low baseline symptoms and greater symptom reduction among those with elevated symptoms. Among participants with elevated baseline symptom severity, Flourish produced higher rates of meaningful change for depressive symptoms (52.3% vs 31.7%; NNT = 4.9) and anxiety symptoms (50.6% vs 38.1%; NNT = 8.0).
Conclusions and Relevance: To our knowledge, this is the largest randomized clinical trial of a conversational AI mental health platform and among the first to demonstrate effectiveness in a population-based college sample recruited without symptom-based screening. These findings support purpose-built generative AI platforms as scalable first-line mental health tools.

articles.title: Negotiating the Good Mother Ideal: Maternal Guilt in Contemporary Society
articles.authors: Maria-Valentina Stoicescu; Natalia Soboleva; Nevena Kulic; Giulia Dotti Sani
articles.abstract: This narrative review integrates quantitative and qualitative research to provide a comprehensive overview of the phenomenon of maternal guilt, examining its measurement, types, triggers, and prevalence. Maternal guilt is a widespread emotion that is associated with the societal role of women as caregivers, yet this association is sometimes lost when talking more broadly about parental guilt. Critical gaps persist in the understanding and measurement of maternal guilt, particularly in the areas of cross-cultural variation, intersectional perspectives, and the long-term consequences for both mothers and their children. The classification of maternal guilt into types highlights that maternal guilt can take different forms, depending on specific root causes and circumstances. By analysing triggers, this review also shows that maternal guilt is enhanced through individual, situational, and institutional factors, and it varies across countries, being influenced by societal and cultural characteristics.

articles.title: Using Statistical Modelling and Geographic Data to Challenge Declinist Headlines - a Case Study of Fish and Chip Shops in Great Britain
articles.authors: Stephen Clark; Andy Newing
articles.abstract: The traditional fish and chip shop is an important part of the food environment in many UK neighbourhoods. However, rising costs, changing tastes and competition have resulted in fish and chip shop closures, with some alarmist predictions that up to half of all shops could close in a single year. Using Points of Interest data and multilevel modelling, we look beyond the headlines and model trends in fish and chip shop provision by neighbourhood type between 2022 and 2025. We identify an overall decline in the number of shops yet find no evidence to support the predictions of widespread closures. However, closures have been unevenly spread across neighbourhood types, with the greatest decline in deprived and ethnically diverse urban areas, and less impact on some more affluent neighbourhoods with older populations. Although some adaption may be needed to maintain viable amidst rising costs, our findings suggest that the traditional fish and chip shop remains an important part of the food environment in many neighbourhoods. In conclusion, we highlight the importance of robust data driven insight when considering the changing provision of services and comment on how these approaches could be applied in other contexts.

articles.title: Teaching Teachers
articles.authors: Danilo Kuzmanic; Per Engzell; John Jerrim
articles.abstract: Good teachers are role models. Using Chilean administrative data, we estimate how teacher value-added (VA) affects the VA of students who later become teachers. Exposure to a math teacher with 1 SD higher VA raises teacher VA in the next generation by 0.14 SD and student achievement one generation removed by 0.04 SD. Effects compound with longer exposure and appear driven by observable classroom practices. Under plausible assumptions, these spillovers raise cumulative returns to instructional quality by 28 percent after one generation and 39 percent in the long run.

articles.title: Peace, War, and the Spirit of Commerce: Genovesi as a reader of Montesquieu
articles.authors: JHET Assistant; Paolo Santori
articles.abstract: What are the consequences of trade among nations? In De l’esprit des lois, Montesquieu claimed that the spirit of commerce fosters peace, restraining conflict by leading nations to collaborate in pursuit of mutual benefit. Economic interests, he suggested, channel and moderate the violent impulses of human passions. In contrast, the Neapolitan philosopher and economist Antonio Genovesi offered a strikingly different interpretation. In his reading of Montesquieu, Genovesi argued that the spirit of commerce was itself a spirit of conquest—an engine of rivalry and domination among nations. Yet, he maintained, the telos of trade rests upon an alternative spirit, one of mutual aid, capable of nurturing peace and cooperation both within and between societies. This paper offers a philosophical analysis of the dialogue between Montesquieu and Genovesi, tracing how their exchange shaped Genovesi’s evolving reflections on the intricate relationship between commerce and war.

articles.title: Industry Differences in Upward Mobility from Low-Wage Labour Market Entry
articles.authors: Karin Kristensson
articles.abstract: A low-wage occupation is a common entry point into the labour market, yet the trajectories that follow vary considerably. This study examines whether patterns of upward mobility from low-wage labour market entry differ across industries. Using Swedish full population register data, the analysis follows all individuals aged 18–25 who entered the labour market in 2003 in low wage occupations and traces their occupational trajectories over 18 years. Event history models are used to estimate the timing of transitions from low wage to higher wage occupations. The results indicate substantial and persistent differences across industries in both the pace and likelihood of upward mobility. Workers in manufacturing and construction tend to move into higher wage occupations more quickly and more often, whereas those in hospitality, cleaning, and personal services experience markedly slower transitions. In several of these service industries, workers remain in low wage occupations more than twice as long as comparable workers in manufacturing, and a sizeable share do not transition at all within the observation period. As these service industries expand their share of low wage jobs—and as women and immigrants remain disproportionately concentrated within them—these patterns raise concerns about social stratification. The findings highlight how industrial structures shape early career mobility and interact with broader labour market inequalities, underscoring the importance of industrial opportunity structures for understanding trajectories from low-wage work.

articles.title: Between the Embedding and the Prompt: Systematic Design Effects in LLM-based Occupation Coding
articles.authors: Olga Kononykhina; Anna-Carolina Haensch; Frauke Kreuter
articles.abstract: Assigning free-text job descriptions to standardised taxonomies is a persistent bottleneck in survey research and official statistics. Large language models (LLMs) offer a promising path toward automation, but each step in the pipeline involves both model architecture and measurement choices about how an occupation should be represented. Through 119 experiments on German survey data, we systematically vary the textual representation of occupational categories, embedding models, LLMs, and prompt design. Category representation changes retrieval accuracy by 8–23 percentage points and classification by 11–21. Prompt role and abstention behaviour are model-specific and must be validated before deployment. The dominant source of variance, however, sits outside model measurement choices. How respondents describe their work matters more than any model or design choice (ICC = 0.76).

articles.title: TOWARD MORPHOLOGICALLY-AWARE TOKENIZATION FOR INFLECTIONAL LANGUAGES: A STRUCTURAL HYPOTHESIS
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article proposes a theoretical hypothesis concerning the structural and temporal architecture of tokenization in inflectional languages. The starting point is an observation documented in prior empirical work (Dawidek, 2026a; 2026b): standard frequency-based tokenization systematically fails to preserve morphologically significant boundaries in Polish, a synthetic inflectional language. The present article argues that this failure has a deeper cause than training data composition or algorithm choice. Standard tokenization has no preconditions — it begins cutting without structural knowledge of the word — and no concept of time: it treats the interval between cuts as empty latency rather than as a potential carrier of morphological information.
I propose two interconnected hypotheses. First, that linguistically motivated tokenization requires a morphological skeleton as a precondition — a minimal representation of root, prefix, ending type, case, number, gender, and valency, specified here for Polish and hypothesized to generalize to other inflectional languages. Second, that the cutting order itself is morphologically motivated and temporally structured: prefix first, inflectional ending second, root exposed last — where exposure is not a cut but a revelation of what was present throughout.
I further propose the concept of morphological resolution time — the interval between cuts understood as a site of grammatical resolution rather than computational waste. This proposal has architectural consequences: the temporal dimension required by the hypothesis is structurally incompatible with standard transformer parallelism. A hybrid architecture combining sequential morphological pre-processing with parallel downstream processing is identified as a research direction.
This article presents a hypothesis, not demonstrated results. It is offered as a theoretical framework for empirical work that has not yet been done — and as a named set of variables that must exist before measurement can begin.

articles.title: Mind the Self-Promotion Gap: Gendered Perceptions and Responses to an Informational Intervention
articles.authors: Maalvika Bhat; Inna Smirnova; Misha Teplitskiy; Agnes Horvat
articles.abstract: Promoting one’s research on social media has become an increasingly important part of academic careers, with implications for career advancement and the circulation of scientific knowledge. Prior research finds that men tend to self-promote their research papers more than women. This study draws on a survey of 3,111 U.S.-based academics to examine why this gender gap persists and experimentally test whether informing participants of the gap influences their intentions to self-promote in the future. We find that women are more likely than men to report reputational benefits (e.g., recognition, networking opportunities) but also higher interpersonal and professional costs (e.g., harassment, credibility loss, and time constraints). Men, by contrast, more often report skepticism about usefulness or audience presence as reasons for not self-promoting. Exposure to information about the gender gap is associated with significantly lower stated intentions among men to promote their next paper on social media. Women’s intentions, by contrast, remain unchanged, consistent with perceived costs of self-promotion, not lack of information about the gap, being the primary barrier among women. Overall, we conclude that increasing women’s intentions to self-promote their research requires systemic changes to evaluation criteria, anti-harassment infrastructure, and institutional support for public engagement.

articles.title: The Public Health Impact of GLP-1 Advertising Exposure in the U.S.
articles.authors: Matt Motta; Timothy Callaghan; Sarah K Lipson; Jennifer Ross; Kiersten Strombotne; Christopher Louis
articles.abstract: The recent discovery that a class of drugs collectively known as “GLP-1s” (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1) can promote weight loss has led to a surge in pharmaceutical production, demand, and advertising. While previous research has considered the impact that advertising exposure may have on public demand for using GLP-1s for weight management, few have considered the broader public health impacts (both positive and negative) of GLP-1 advertising. For example, increased GLP-1 advertisement exposure could make people more self-conscious about their own weight and physical appearance. It could also promote destigmatizing views toward those who use GLP-1 products for weight management. In a nationally representative survey of N = 1,000 US adults, we find that people exposed to higher levels of GLP-1 advertising tend to hold more negative views about their own weight and physical appearance. We find no evidence that ad exposure is associated with destigmatizing views about those who use GLP-1 products, although we find strong evidence that exposure can stimulate demand for GLP-1 products. These results hold across a wide range of measures and model specifications. We conclude by discussing the potential public health risks of GLP-1 advertising, in a changing regulatory landscape.

articles.title: Social Entropy, Adaptive Coherence, and Transcendent Detachment: An Integrative Framework for Measuring Coherence and Fragility in Human Systems
articles.authors: Sherif Rashwan
articles.abstract:
Human groups, institutions, relationships, and identities can be modeled as dynamic information-processing systems that require continual maintenance to pre
serve coherence under uncertainty. This article develops Adaptive Coherence and Entropic Attachment (ACEA), an integrative framework linking information theory, cybernetics, resilience science, attachment theory, stress appraisal, predictive processing, allostasis, discourse analysis, and institutional theory. The framework formalizes social coherence as a time-dependent state variable shaped by trust, shared knowledge, relational cohesion, communication clarity, adaptive capacity, regenerative repair, informational noise, fear, uncertainty, external disturbance, and attachment rigidity. The central contribution is a testable account of “transcendent detachment” as adaptive non-rigidity: the capacity to preserve meaning, commitment, and identity without requiring absolute permanence or total control. ACEA is not presented as a finished empirical law, but as a formal research program that generates measurable constructs, falsifiable hypotheses, and practical applications for discourse analysis, organizational resilience, conflict mediation, institutional design, and psychological adaptation.

articles.title: The Origin of Patriarchal Moral System: An Evolutionary Model
articles.authors: Maksim Sapozhnikov
articles.abstract:
[This is a new submission replacing a previous version that was accidentally deleted. The earlier submission was declined due to an incomplete ORCID profile; the profile has now been updated with full professional information, qualifications, and biography. The manuscript is currently under peer review at the journal Human Nature and is not AI-generated.]

This article proposes an evolutionary-materialist model for the origin of the patriarchal moral system — a set of normative prohibitions restricting divorce, female sexuality, and freedom of mate choice. The model takes as its starting point the reproductive strategy of male provisioning — the exchange of male‑provided resources for female reproductive access — shaped by sexual selection. It is shown that the transition from foraging to civilization generates three demographic problems that make this strategy unstable: (a) increased population density facilitating partner switching, (b) awareness of the causal link between intercourse and childbirth reducing female motivation for sex, and (c) dissociation between the timing of sexual and social maturity in males. The normative solution to each of these problems—and their combination as a patriarchal moral system—is proposed to be understood as an adaptation to new demographic conditions. The model explains the convergent emergence of similar prohibitions across different cultures and the specific role of older males in enforcing them. The article is theoretical in nature and can serve as a foundation for further empirical research.

articles.title: Naming under constraint: gender, care and symbolic power in family life
articles.authors: Patrícia Albergaria Almeida
articles.abstract:
This article proposes a reconceptualisation of children’s surname attribution as a gendered site of symbolic power within heterosexual family life. While naming is commonly framed as a private and consensual decision, feminist scholarship has shown that family practices are structured by persistent asymmetries of care, authority, and recognition. Yet the symbolic dimension of naming remains underexamined.
Building on feminist theories of care, symbolic violence, and situated knowledge, this article introduces the concept of naming under constraint to analyse how naming decisions are produced under conditions that limit deliberation and agency. Drawing on a case in Portugal, it shows how a naming decision taken immediately after childbirth - under bodily vulnerability, institutional urgency, and unequal care relations - becomes stabilised as a consensual outcome.
The article further develops the notion of delayed articulation to theorise the temporal gap between living inequality and naming it as such, highlighting how care-intensive life stages constrain feminist critique.
By shifting attention from naming as a neutral outcome to naming as a situated process, this article contributes to sociological debates on gender, care, and symbolic power, and highlights the importance of attending to the temporal and relational conditions under which inequality becomes intelligible.

articles.title: Acceptance of transport policies:  A factorial survey experiment in  the German Longitudinal  Environmental Study (GLEN)
articles.authors: Christiane Bozoyan; Claudia Schmiedeberg; Henning Best
articles.abstract: Due to the significant negative externalities associated with the transport sector, policies that effectively address these issues and guide the transition to a low-carbon transport system are required. In order to gain insight into the level of public support for a number of transport policy interventions currently under debate in Germany, a factorial survey experiment on interventions to promote the transition from combustion engine vehicles to battery electric vehicles (BEVs) was conducted as part of the GLEN starter survey in January/February 2025. This report documents the experiment.

articles.title: A New Approach to Addressing the Rampant Gun Violence in the United States
articles.authors: 周勇
articles.abstract: A ban on ammunition would prohibit the private possession and circulation of ammunition and specialized components used in its manufacture, while allowing government-authorized and regulated commercial shooting ranges and hunting outfitters to possess ammunition and conduct lawful shooting activities. It would also permit the private collection of certified top-tier antique ammunition, and permits reloading enthusiasts to reload ammunition in government-authorized and regulated reloading workshops, while providing a service to deliver the reloaded ammunition to their designated commercial shooting ranges or hunting outfitters. The government subsidizes commercial shooting ranges, hunting outfitters, and reloading workshops, enabling them to offer their primary services free of charge to consumers. The new policy implements robust regulatory measures for ammunition in civilian circulation, and repeals all existing gun control laws and regulations, allowing anyone to freely own and carry firearms, and explicitly states that no background checks will be conducted on consumers entering commercial shooting ranges, hunting outfitters, or reloading workshops. The new policy has a minimal impact on the firearms industry’s revenue (8%) and does not increase the government’s fiscal burden (annual subsidies account for 0.2% of federal revenue). The new policy significantly reduces the cost of sport shooting (by 26%) and high-end hunting (by 9%), while causing virtually no harm to the public’s shooting rights (affected shooting activities account for only 10% of those prior to the policy). The new policy benefits 24% of all adults, while only 4% of adults are negatively affected, and will pass smoothly in both the House and Senate with approximately 85% support. This widespread public support will prompt the Supreme Court to issue a new judicial interpretation of the Second Amendment, ensuring the smooth implementation of the new policy.

articles.title: Panel Study of Russian Public Opinion and Attitudes (PROPA) Wave 4
articles.authors: Aleksei Gilev; Marina Vyrskaia; Ilia Nadporozhskii; Tommaso Valastro; Margarita Zavadskaya
articles.abstract:
This fourth wave of the Panel Study of Russian Public Opinion and Attitudes (PROPA), conducted in June 2025, provides new insights into how Russians view the economy, the war in Ukraine, political institutions, propaganda, and the country’s future.
Economic perceptions remain broadly stable. Most respondents report that their household income enables them to cover basic and some discretionary expenses, although inflation remains a persistent concern. Evaluations of both personal and national economic conditions have neither improved nor sharply deteriorated, yet perceptions of inflation and limited growth continue to pose risks to regime legitimacy.
Attitudes toward the war show a gradual but consistent erosion of open support for the “special military operation,” falling from 52% to 48% since March 2024. Meanwhile, open opposition has grown modestly, and support for peace talks has increased. War fatigue, the spillover of drone attacks into Russian cities, and the disruption of daily life appear to be contributing factors. At the same time, many respondents remain ambivalent or unwilling to state their position, reflecting ongoing fear and censorship.
Media consumption patterns highlight the effectiveness of state censorship: YouTube usage has dropped under state throttling, while reliance on Telegram for news has increased. Television remains dominant but shows signs of eroding trust. Political interest remains relatively high, though open discussion of politics has slightly declined, reflecting awareness of repression.
Approval of political institutions continues to edge downward. Support for the State Duma and government has weakened, and even Vladimir Putin’s approval shows a modest but consistent decline. Although Putin retains support from more than half of respondents, the rally-around-the-flag effect has clearly waned. Institutions such as the police and courts enjoy particularly low moral authority, while the army and the Russian Orthodox Church remain relatively trusted.
Expectations for the future are mixed. While most respondents continue to anticipate economic growth and see renewed mobilization as unlikely, optimism about a quick peace settlement has declined following unmet expectations of U.S.-mediated negotiations. Concerns about rising crime, linked to the reintegration of war veterans, are growing.
Overall, the data suggest a society that is economically stable but politically fragile. War weariness, limited trust in institutions, and declining approval of state actors all indicate that the regime’s legitimacy depends increasingly on managing perceptions of economic stability and avoiding sharp shocks that could alter public expectations.

articles.title: 《逻辑的自我反噬：论波普尔证伪论的内在悖论》 The Self-Refutation of Logic: On the Internal Paradoxes of Popper’s Falsificationism
articles.authors: songping zeng
articles.abstract:
Karl Popper’s falsificationism, which takes falsifiability as the demarcation criterion between science and non‑science, attempts to overcome the logical predicament of induction. Yet his own theory harbors irresolvable internal paradoxes. This paper systematically analyzes the multiple paradoxes of Popper’s falsificationism from three levels: its core criterion, the demarcation standard, and logical derivation. First, the core principle of falsificationism is itself not falsifiable, leading to logical self‑refutation and thus losing its legitimacy as a demarcation criterion. Second, the demarcation standard suffers from double standards, blurring the boundaries between common‑sense facts, metaphysical superstitions, and scientific propositions, thereby rendering the criterion completely ineffective. Third, it severs the connection between imaginary (logically possible) falsifiability and real (empirically possible) falsifiability, divorcing itself from the actual practice of science and degenerating into a mere logical word‑game. Popper’s falsifiability criterion deviates from the truth‑seeking essence of science, overturns the commonsense logic of induction that uses real counterexamples to refute false conclusions, classifies empirically refuted false propositions as “scientific”, and even leads to the absurd conclusion that the more counterexamples a theory has (the higher its falsifiability), the more “scientific” it becomes. It thus severs the link between a theory’s truth or falsehood and its scientific status, exposing the inherent defect of formalist logic divorced from objective empirical evidence.
The paper argues that Popper’s falsificationism is essentially a metaphysical philosophical dogma rather than a rigorous demarcation criterion for science. Its simplistic, one‑sided logical standard cannot achieve the core task of demarcating science, and ultimately collapses into self‑defeating logical inconsistency. A genuine demarcation of science must be grounded in practice, balance logic with empirical evidence, and adopt a pluralistic, comprehensive approach.

卡尔•波普尔提出的证伪主义以可证伪性作为科学与非科学的划界标准，试图突破归纳法的逻辑困境，但其理论自身潜藏着无法消解的内在悖论。本文从核心准则、划界标准、逻辑推演三个层面，系统剖析波普尔证伪论的多重悖论：其一，证伪论核心原则自身不具备可证伪性，陷入逻辑自我反噬，失去判定准则的合法性；其二，划界标准存在双重标准，混淆客观常识、玄学迷信与科学命题的边界，导致标准彻底失效；其三，割裂逻辑假想可证伪性与现实可证伪性，脱离科学实践本质，沦为纯粹逻辑文字游戏。波普尔证伪性划界标准背离科学求真的本质，颠覆了归纳法以真实反例否定谬误结论的常识逻辑，将已被事实证伪的错误命题归入科学范畴，甚至形成反例越多、可证伪性越强则越“科学”的倒置结论，既割裂了理论正误与科学属性的关联，也暴露了其形式化逻辑脱离客观实证的固有缺陷。
本研究认为，波普尔证伪论本质是形而上学哲学信条，而非严谨科学判定准则，其以偏概全的单一逻辑标准无法实现科学划界的核心目标，最终在自我否定的逻辑困局中走向失效。真正的科学划界需立足实践、兼顾逻辑与经验，实现多元综合判定。

articles.title: The Silence of Moderates Drives Social Polarization: An Agent-Based Model of Perceived and Actual Polarization
articles.authors: Kazuya Iwata; Hiroshi Shimizu
articles.abstract: Social polarization increasingly undermines the collaborative dialogue essential to democratic societies, necessitating a clearer understanding of the mechanisms behind its emergence and resolution. Traditional research has explained polarization through interpersonal repulsion, in which individuals move their opinions away from different views (e.g., Axelrod et al., 2021). While this framework focuses on conflicts among active speakers, it overlooks the silence and withdrawal of moderates. We developed a model that excludes repulsion and incorporates the silence gap effect, in which individuals remain silent toward those with stronger attitudes. Simulations show that in the early stages, the silence gap effect causes moderates to remain silent while allowing extreme opinions to dominate public expressions. This generates perceived polarization, a state in which the distribution of public opinion becomes bimodal despite unimodal internal attitudes. Subsequently, the frequently expressed extreme opinions pull other agents toward the poles, eventually leading to polarization. These findings demonstrate that the essence of polarization lies in the silence of moderates. Furthermore, our study shows that polarization can be prevented by neutralizing the silence gap effect, ensuring that moderates do not suppress opinions. However, we found that even slight differences in intervention timing determined whether future polarization was successfully averted. By contrast, interventions aimed at correcting the misperception of a polarized public failed to prevent division. Therefore, to resolve polarization, it is vital to create a dialogue environment in which moderates can speak as early as possible without apprehension, rather than directly addressing extreme voices or promoting the correction of public perception.

articles.title: Image Abstraction and Bayesian Inference: A Reconstruction of the Nature of Probability Theory from the Perspective of Cognitive Philosophy
articles.authors: Chen Lijun
articles.abstract:
This paper demonstrates, from an epistemological standpoint, the structural isomorphism between Quxiang Bilei—the classical Chinese cognitive method of image abstraction and analogical reasoning, literally "taking phenomena to form analogies and categorical associations"—and Bayesian inference. By systematically mapping the three components of Bayes's theorem—prior probability, likelihood function, and posterior probability—onto the three constitutive stages of Quxiang Bilei: image-taking, analogy-making, and verification, this paper reveals that Bayesian inference is not merely a specialized technical toolset, but rather the mathematical formalization of the metacognitive structure inherent in Quxiang Bilei.
Building upon this isomorphic framework, the paper advances five core philosophical propositions: (1) objectivity is the statistical convergence outcome of collective Bayesian updating across epistemic communities; (2) causal laws represent the limiting case in which posterior probability infinitely approaches unity; (3) the interpretability of probabilistic calculations resides not within mathematics itself, but in its correspondence with underlying metacognitive structures; (4) the absolute boundary of artificial intelligence lies not in insufficient computational power, but in its lack of embodied physical anchoring and authentic survival feedback; and (5) there exists a fundamental, non-accidental isomorphism between Quxiang Bilei and Bayesian inference.
These five propositions are not mere mutual projections between two disparate conceptual frameworks, but genuinely novel insights emerging from their productive dialogue and critical engagement. This work provides a robust epistemological foundation for Bayesianism, offers rigorous mathematical support for the modern transformation of classical Chinese epistemology, and establishes a unified analytical framework for understanding the cognitive essence and fundamental limitations of artificial intelligence.

articles.title: Toward a Framework for Digital Cultural Pedagogy: A Systematic Review of Local Wisdom and Cultural Identity Education
articles.authors: Marwan Marwan
articles.abstract: The integration of digital learning materials into local wisdom and cultural identity education has emerged as a significant focus in educational technology research, particularly in developing-country contexts where globalization threatens the transmission of indigenous and regional knowledge systems. This systematic literature review (SLR) synthesizes empirical evidence on the effectiveness, design principles, and theoretical underpinnings of digital teaching materials for local wisdom literacy and cultural identity formation among primary and secondary school students, with particular attention to Indonesian and Southeast Asian educational contexts. Following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, a systematic search was conducted across five electronic databases (Scopus, Web of Science, ERIC, Google Scholar, and DOAJ) covering the period 2014–2025. Of 1,247 initially identified records, 42 studies met the inclusion criteria following full-text screening and quality assessment using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT). Synthesized evidence demonstrates that digitally enhanced, culturally grounded teaching materials consistently produce significant improvements in local wisdom literacy and measurable gains in cultural identity indicators. Four design principles emerged as critical determinants of effectiveness: (1) cultural authenticity validation, (2) multimodal content integration, (3) ADDIE-based systematic development, and (4) scaffolded cultural identity engagement. Vygotsky’s socio-cultural constructivism, Mayer’s Cognitive Theory of Multimedia Learning, and Bandura’s Social Cognitive Theory constitute the most commonly applied theoretical frameworks. Rigorously developed digital teaching materials represent evidence-based, scalable instruments for culturally responsive pedagogy. Beyond synthesizing empirical evidence, this review proposes a conceptual architecture for digital cultural pedagogy integrating cultural authenticity validation, multimodal learning representation, scaffolded identity engagement, and iterative instructional evaluation. The framework provides a theoretically grounded and evidence-based foundation for culturally responsive digital education, cultural sustainability, and future curriculum innovation across diverse educational settings.

articles.title: The Preprint Problem Problem: Response to Giangrande
articles.authors: Philip N. Cohen
articles.abstract: Giangrande (2026) argues that fringe scientists have “weaponized” preprints to spread pseudoscience, and presents data to prove the point. In a response, I identify two major problems in the paper. The first is that the data do not match their description, and the second is that description of how preprint servers work – and thus the mechanism of action for the problem – is inaccurate. In addition, the documentation provided with the paper is inadequate for assessment. The paper does not establish the prevalence of weaponized pseudoscience on preprint servers beyond an existence proof. I conclude that the paper cannot live up to its inflammatory title, “The Preprint Problem” – but scientists do have an important role to play in protecting our communities from the bad actors of pseudoscience.

articles.title: Finite-Capacity Prospect Theory: State-Dependent Risk Preferences under Resource, Attention, and Boundary-Risk Constraints
articles.authors: Yining Wu
articles.abstract:
This paper develops a state-dependent version of Prospect Theory in which loss aversion, reference adaptation, and probability weighting vary with resource buffers, attention constraints, and boundary-risk context. Prospect Theory is usually treated as a descriptive account of deviations from expected-utility rationality. This paper proposes a finite-capacity bridge model: bounded agents operating under scarcity, limited attention, finite memory, and finite update capacity should exhibit state-dependent risk preferences.

The model introduces a vector-valued resource buffer, perceived distance to a constraint threshold, event-class-dependent probability weighting, and a nudge-bandwidth model. It predicts that loss aversion should rise under scarcity and downside exposure, reference baselines should adapt more slowly under depletion, and probability weighting should become more distorted for low-capacity agents and boundary-relevant rare events.

The paper includes deterministic synthetic simulations and a replication package, but does not fit human-subject data. It is intended as a theoretical working paper and empirical identification framework for future tests of state-dependent Prospect Theory.

articles.title: Chinese Automotive Finance: Development, Policies, and Regulations
articles.authors: kerry liu
articles.abstract: The rapid development of Chinese automotive, particularly in the new energy vehicle sector, have garnered global attention. This study focuses on a niche area that has yet to be thoroughly examined: automotive finance. First, it explores the evolution of automotive finance in China within the context of the broader automotive market. Second, it reviews key policy initiatives from Chinese authorities related to the automotive industry, automotive finance companies, and automotive consumption. Third, it analyzes recent regulations on automotive finance companies, concluding that these measures are designed to mitigate systemic risks within China's financial system while supporting the new energy vehicle sector.

articles.title: Beyond the Takeover: Youth Gatherings, Urban Public Space, and the Racialization of Disorder in Chicago
articles.authors: Almethia C. Franklin
articles.abstract:
In recent years, Chicago has witnessed a significant rise in large-scale youth gatherings in high-visibility public spaces. Labeled "teen takeovers" in dominant media and policy discourse, these events are almost exclusively framed as public safety threats requiring law enforcement response. This paper argues that this framing is both analytically insufficient and politically consequential. Drawing on preliminary media and news analysis, an emerging qualitative research agenda, and a multi-disciplinary theoretical framework, this paper reframes youth gatherings as social phenomena that reveal fundamental dynamics of race, space, territorial boundary, and urban belonging. We identify three dimensions that existing scholarship has not yet systematically examined: (1) the role of social media and digital technology in organizing, escalating, and documenting these events; (2) the apparent dissolution of hyperlocal territorial boundaries historically maintained by street organizations in Chicago; and (3) the variation in form, activity, and framing of analogous youth gatherings across U.S. cities. We situate these dimensions within a five-part theoretical architecture drawing on moral panic theory, critical criminology, spatial theory, Black cultural studies, and the sociology of punishment. We conclude by presenting a research agenda for empirical investigation and discussing the implications of this work for policy and community response.

Keywords: youth gatherings, teen takeovers, urban public space, moral panic, territorial boundaries, Chicago, racialization, social media, cultural criminology

articles.title: How does Coethnicity with Refugees Shape their Reception? Evidence from Afghan Refugees in Pakistan
articles.authors: Mashail Malik; Niloufer Siddiqui; Yang-Yang Zhou
articles.abstract: How does coethnicity shape host attitudes toward refugees? Existing research finds that cultural threat drives anti-refugee attitudes, so refugees who share an ethnic background with host populations should face less backlash. We examine this in Pakistan, where both refugee and host communities include substantial Pashtun populations.
